react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/01-the-problem.md: 'Goal: read this lesson without writing a single line of code...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: read this lesson without writing a single line of code, and end with a clear, written-down understanding of what we are about to build and why.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 'Goal: translate yesterday's pub conversation into precise Ty...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: translate yesterday's pub conversation into precise TypeScript types. By the end of this lesson you'll have written lib/types.ts — the single most important file in the codebase, even though it doesn't render anything.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 'For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or team.r2, qf,...'
react-snooker.back: For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or team.r2, qf, sf) corresponds to the i-th element of ROUND1MATCHES (or ROUND2MATCHES, QFMATCHES, SFMATCHES).

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Why winner? is optional?
react-snooker.back: This is the fork in the road that powers the whole app. Re-read Lesson 1's scoring rules: a match that hasn't been played has null (or, equivalently here, undefined) for every team's score. The data-level mechanism that produces that behaviour is the absence of match.winner.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Why score? and seed1? are optional?
react-snooker.back: score is optional because finished matches have one and pending matches don't. Same logic as winner.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 1. r1, r2, qf, sf are arrays of strings — but final is just a string?
react-snooker.back: Lesson 1 reminded us there's only one Final match. So a team only makes one Final pick. We could have written final: string[] with one element, but that would cost us readability everywhere we touch it: team.final[0] === 'Wu Yize' reads worse than team.final === 'Wu Yize'. So we accept the asymmetry. The cost is that scoring code has one branch for Final and another for everything else (we'll see this in Lesson 3).

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 2. The cosmetics live next to the picks?
react-snooker.back: color, accent, icon, motto are decorative. They drive every gradient, badge, and chip in the UI. They live on the same object as the picks because each team is one logical thing — not two (a "team identity" plus "team picks"). Splitting them would buy nothing and add a level of indirection.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: 3. r1Players? and r2Players? exist for a subtle scoring case?
react-snooker.back: These aren't needed for normal scoring — they're a hint used by calculateTeamScores when a team's pick advances through the round in a non-obvious way (e.g. the team picked someone in their R1 spot, but the R2 spot is for a different bracket position than they expected). For most teams these arrays are unused. They're optional precisely because most teams won't have them.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/02-data-modeling.md: Answer to #1 (because it's a real gotcha)?
react-snooker.back: winner?: string and winner: string | undefined look almost the same, but they differ on whether the field has to be specified at all when constructing the object.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: 'Goal: write lib/scoring.ts — two pure functions, no React, n...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: write lib/scoring.ts — two pure functions, no React, no JSX, no DOM. By the end of this lesson the heart of the application is beating, and you haven't typed import React even once.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: 'Interview tip. When asked "how do you structure a React app?...'
react-snooker.back: Interview tip. When asked "how do you structure a React app?", the seniors say "separate pure data/logic from rendering." If you can point to your own lib/ folder full of pure functions and your components/ folder of pure presentation, you've got a working answer with proof.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: 'If the match isn't finished, return null. Otherwise, return ...'
react-snooker.back: If the match isn't finished, return null. Otherwise, return 3 if the pick won, 1 otherwise.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: Branch 1: if (!match || !match.winner) return null;?
react-snooker.back: This is the "pending" branch. Two ways the function arrives here:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: Branch 2: if (pick === match.winner) return 3;?
react-snooker.back: If we reach this line, we know match.winner is a string (TypeScript narrowed it from the previous early-return). If the team's pick matches the winner, they get 3 points.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: Branch 3: return 1;?
react-snooker.back: The match has a winner, but the pick wasn't them. The team gets a consolation point. Snooker's hard; you tried.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: The whole function in one breath?
react-snooker.back: > "If the match isn't finished, return null. Otherwise, return 3 if the pick won, 1 otherwise."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: Round 1 walkthrough?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const r1Details: ScoreDetail[] = team.r1.map((pick, i) => { const pts = scorePick(pick, ROUND1MATCHES[i]); r1 += pts || 0; return { match: ROUND1MATCHES[i], pick, points: pts, correct: pts === null ? null : pts === 3, }; }); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: The Final is special?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const finalMatch = FINAL_MATCH[0]; const fPts = scorePick(team.final, finalMatch); f += fPts || 0; const fDetails: ScoreDetail[] = [{ match: finalMatch, pick: team.final, points: fPts, correct: fPts === null ? null : fPts === 3, }]; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/03-pure-functions-and-scoring.md: The total and the return?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const total = r1 + r2 + qf + sf + f; return { r1, r2, qf, sf, f, total, r1Details, r2Details, qfDetails, sfDetails, fDetails }; } ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-01-foundations/README.md: 'Before you write a single line of React, you need to underst...'
react-snooker.back: Before you write a single line of React, you need to understand the problem and the data. Half of senior interviews are "how would you model this?" — and that's a question that's answered before any JSX gets typed.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'Goal: understand the framework decision tree well enough to ...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: understand the framework decision tree well enough to defend "we used Next.js" in an interview without looking nervous. Beginners pick frameworks by vibe; seniors pick by requirements.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'Interview answer template:* "Compile-time errors, self-docum...'
react-snooker.back: Interview answer template:* "Compile-time errors, self-documenting types, and editor support. The cost is a small upfront learning curve and slightly more verbose code, but for any project bigger than a 50-line script the win is overwhelming.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 'I'm building a single-page React app for a fantasy sports le...'
react-snooker.back: I'm building a single-page React app for a fantasy sports league. ~250 hand-typed data records, 5 tabs, no backend, no auth, no real-time, deployment to Vercel. Should I use Next.js or Vite + React? Lay out the tradeoffs honestly. Don't just say 'use the popular one.'

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: Vite wins on:?
react-snooker.back: - Dev server startup speed. Vite boots in ~200ms; Next takes 2-3 seconds. - Less framework magic. What you see in your code is what runs. - Smaller bundle by default. No SSR overhead, no Next runtime. - Easier to migrate to a non-React framework later. Vite is generic.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: Next.js wins on:?
react-snooker.back: - Vercel deployment with literally one click. Push to GitHub, import on Vercel, done. Vite needs a hosting target you configure (Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, etc.). - TypeScript + Tailwind + ESLint pre-wired. npx create-next-app gives you a working project with all of these. With Vite you assemble them yourself. - Image and font optimization out of the box (next/font, next/image). - Routing if you ever expand. If next year you add a /leaderboard/[year] route, Next has it free; Vite needs react-router-dom. - Server components for free. Even though we won't use them heavily, the option is there.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 2. Self-documenting code?
react-snooker.back: When you write function calculateTeamScores(team: Team): TeamScores, anyone reading the code knows the input shape, the output shape, and they can Cmd+Click to navigate to either. Types are documentation that the compiler keeps honest. Comments drift. Types don't.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 3. Editor superpowers?
react-snooker.back: VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf — every modern editor turns into a god-tier autocomplete machine when you have types. You type team. and it shows you r1, r2, qf, sf, final, .... You don't have to memorize the shape; the editor reminds you.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: When NOT to use TypeScript?
react-snooker.back: There's a reasonable counterargument for tiny scripts (under 50 lines), prototypes you'll throw away, and learning exercises where the type ceremony obscures the lesson. For anything shipped to production, multi-file, or maintained by more than one person — TypeScript wins.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 1. To set up globals.css properly?
react-snooker.back: app/globals.css has these three Tailwind directives at the top:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 2. To leave the door open for future Tailwind use?
react-snooker.back: If you later want to convert one component from inline styles to Tailwind classes, the toolchain's already set up. No re-config needed.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: 3. So next/font and other Next.js features that integrate with Tailwind work cleanly?
react-snooker.back: Some Next plugins assume Tailwind is present. Having it costs ~5KB of CSS and zero developer time.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/01-why-nextjs-typescript.md: Why inline styles instead of Tailwind classes??
react-snooker.back: The codebase uses dynamic, computed styles based on team colors, gradients with multiple custom values, and conditional logic that would produce very long class strings if rendered with Tailwind:

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: 'Goal: from an empty folder to a running Next.js dev server, ...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: from an empty folder to a running Next.js dev server, with every config file explained line by line. By the end of this lesson, npm run dev boots a placeholder page at http://localhost:3000.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: 'Why does npm run dev need run but npm start doesn't? Histori...'
react-snooker.back: Why does npm run dev need run but npm start doesn't? Historical: a few script names (start, test, install, restart, stop) are built-in shortcuts; the rest need run. It's a wart. Live with it.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: "name", "version", "private"?
react-snooker.back: - "name" is the project name. Used by tools, never by your code. - "version" follows semver (semantic versioning). Doesn't matter for an unpublished app; matters a lot for npm packages. - "private": true" is a safety latch. It tells npm "do not publish this to the public registry." Don't omit it. Many beginners have accidentally published their unfinished apps because they didn't have this.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: "scripts" — the npm command surface?
react-snooker.back: These are the commands you'll type 100 times during development:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: "devDependencies" — what's needed only at dev/build time?
react-snooker.back: These are tooling: TypeScript itself, type definitions for React/Node, Tailwind/PostCSS, ESLint, Prettier. They don't ship in the production bundle.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/02-scaffolding-the-project.md: app/layout.tsx?
react-snooker.back: ```tsx import type { Metadata } from 'next'; import { Roboto, Roboto_Slab } from 'next/font/google'; import './globals.css';

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: 'Goal: fill out lib/matches.ts, lib/teams.ts, lib/players.ts,...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: fill out lib/matches.ts, lib/teams.ts, lib/players.ts, and lib/constants.ts. By the end of this lesson, the data layer is complete and calculateTeamScores(team) produces real numbers.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: 1. Real apps spend a lot of time on data plumbing?
react-snooker.back: In an interview, you'll be asked to build a feature in 45 minutes. A third of that time is going to be typing fixture data so you have something to render. Practicing it on a real, larger-than-toy dataset prepares you for that.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: 2. Typing the data forces you to feel the invariants?
react-snooker.back: When you write a Team object and have to lay out its 16 R1 picks in the same order as the 16 matches in ROUND1_MATCHES — you internalize Lesson 1.2's invariant in your fingers. That's worth more than reading about it three times.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: export const ROUND1_MATCHES: Match[]?
react-snooker.back: - export so other files (lib/scoring.ts, components) can import it. - const because we never reassign the array. (We could mutate the array's contents, but TypeScript's readonly modifiers can prevent that too — we don't bother for an internal data file.) - : Match[] is the type annotation: an array of Match objects. TypeScript will complain if you forget a required field on any item, or add a typo, or pass a number where a string is expected. That's the whole point.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: The objects themselves?
react-snooker.back: ``ts { id: 1, p1: 'Zhao Xintong', p2: 'Liam Highfield', winner: 'Zhao Xintong', score: '10-7', seed1: 1 }, ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Pending matches look different?
react-snooker.back: By the end of the tournament every match has a winner. But during the tournament, before any QF matches were finished, the QF_MATCHES array would have looked like this:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: The four other rounds?
react-snooker.back: ``ts export const ROUND2MATCHES: Match[] = [ /* 8 entries */ ]; export const QFMATCHES: Match[] = [ / 4 entries / ]; export const SFMATCHES: Match[] = [ /* 2 entries */ ]; export const FINALMATCH: Match[] = [ / 1 entry / ]; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: How the file ends?
react-snooker.back: The full file is about 90 lines. It exports five constants. The order is R1 first, Final last — chronological order from the user's point of view, not bracket-evaluation order. The reader's brain is happier in chronological order.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: What's in each team?
react-snooker.back: - Identity: name, color, accent, icon, motto. The color and accent are the team's primary brand pair, used for gradients in headers and badges. - Picks: r1 (16 strings), r2 (8 strings), qf (4 strings), sf (2 strings), final (1 string).

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: How to type 16 picks without going insane?
react-snooker.back: Realistically you do this with a printout of the bracket on one side and your editor on the other. For each match in ROUND1_MATCHES, ask the team owner "who do you pick?" and write the name in the corresponding slot.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Color choices?
react-snooker.back: Each team has a primary color and an accent. The convention used in this codebase:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Two optional fields you might see?
react-snooker.back: Recall from Lesson 1.2 that Team has two optional helper arrays: r1Players? and r2Players?. You'll see them on a few teams. They're only used in edge cases for scoring. Most of the eight teams will not have these arrays. Don't worry about them yet; you'll meet them again in Chapter 4 if at all.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Record<string, PlayerInfo> explained?
react-snooker.back: Record<K, V> is TypeScript shorthand for "an object whose keys are of type K and values are of type V." Record<string, PlayerInfo> means "any string key is allowed; every value must be a PlayerInfo."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Country flag emojis?
react-snooker.back: Yes, you can put flag emojis directly in TypeScript strings. UTF-8 encoding handles them natively. Just paste them in:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Seeds: top-16 only?
react-snooker.back: ``ts 'Liam Highfield': { country: '🏴', status: 'Qualifier', flag: 'ENG' }, // no seed ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: Why a dictionary, not an array?
react-snooker.back: Already covered in Lesson 1.2. The most common operation is "given a player's name, get their metadata" — that's an O(1) hash lookup with a dictionary, or an O(n) .find() with an array. The dictionary wins on both performance and readability.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: BALL_COLORS — the seven snooker ball colors?
In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: th and td — shared table cell styles?
react-snooker.back: ``ts export const th: React.CSSProperties = { padding: '14px 16px', / ... / }; export const td: React.CSSProperties = { padding: '12px 8px', / ... / }; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/03-creating-data-files.md: tabStyle — a function that returns a style object?
react-snooker.back: ``ts export function tabStyle(active: boolean): React.CSSProperties { ... } ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-02-project-setup/README.md: 'From an empty folder to a running dev server. No React yet —...'
react-snooker.back: From an empty folder to a running dev server. No React yet — but by the end you'll have a Next.js + TypeScript + Tailwind app booting at http://localhost:3000, with your lib/ folder full of typed data and pure scoring logic.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: 'Goal: by the end of this lesson, you can look at any React c...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: by the end of this lesson, you can look at any React component file and identify every part of it — the function, the props, the JSX, the return, the imports — and explain what each one does to a junior developer.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Functional vs class components — the 2026 answer?
react-snooker.back: If you've read any React tutorial older than 2020, you've probably seen class MyComponent extends React.Component { render() { ... } }. That's the class component style. It still works, but you should never write a new class component in 2026.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Why does this matter??
react-snooker.back: Three implications fall out of "JSX is just function calls":

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Lines 3–9: the props interface?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx interface StatCardProps { icon: LucideIcon; label: string; value: string | number; color: string; bgColor: string; } ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Line 11: the function declaration?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx export default function StatCard({ icon: Icon, label, value, color, bgColor }: StatCardProps) { ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Lines 12–32: the return — JSX?
react-snooker.back:
``tsx return (
{label}
{value}
); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: The inline style attribute?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx <div style={{ background: linear-gradient(135deg, ${bgColor} 0%, ${bgColor}DD 100%), color: '#FFFFFF', padding: 24, borderRadius: 16, boxShadow: 0 8px 24px ${bgColor}40, }} > ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/01-anatomy-of-a-component.md: Template literals for dynamic colors?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx background: linear-gradient(135deg, ${bgColor} 0%, ${bgColor}DD 100%), ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: 'Goal: build the first real, data-driven React component in t...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: build the first real, data-driven React component in this app — <StandingsTab> v1. By the end of this lesson, you'll see a sortable table of 8 fantasy teams in your browser, ranked by total points.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: 'Interview question: "What does the key prop do?" The wrong a...'
react-snooker.back: Interview question: "What does the key prop do?" The wrong answer is "it makes the warning go away." The right answer is "it tells React the identity of each list item across renders, so React can reconcile the DOM correctly when the list changes."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: 'use client' at the top — finally?
react-snooker.back: This is the directive we've been foreshadowing. Putting 'use client' as the first line of a file marks the file (and everything it imports that's not also marked server) as a client component. That means it gets shipped to the browser as JavaScript and can use useState, useEffect, event handlers, etc.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: Stub style — minimal so the data is the focus?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx <div style={{ padding: 32, fontFamily: 'sans-serif' }}> ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: Line 1–2: imports?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx import { TEAMS } from ' import { calculateTeamScores } from ' ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: Line 5–8: derive the data?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const teamsWithScores = TEAMS.map(t => ({ ...t, scores: calculateTeamScores(t), })).sort((a, b) => b.scores.total - a.scores.total); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: Lines 11–22: the JSX?
react-snooker.back:
``tsx return ( {teamsWithScores.map((team, i) => ( ))}
RankTeamTotal
{i + 1} {team.name} {team.scores.total}
); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: What is React doing??
react-snooker.back: When the data changes — say, a team's score goes up and the order shifts — React needs to figure out which DOM nodes to keep, which to update, and which to throw away. Without keys, React falls back to comparing by position in the array: the first row is the first row, the second is the second, etc.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/02-rendering-the-standings.md: The classic key bug?
react-snooker.back: Beginners often write key={i} because the index is right there. It works right up until the list reorders or has an item inserted in the middle, at which point React applies updates to the wrong rows and you get bizarre bugs (form inputs lose their values, animations attach to the wrong row, etc.).

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: 'Goal: turn the ugly v1 standings table into the polished ver...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: turn the ugly v1 standings table into the polished version you see in the finished app — by adding one feature at a time. By the end you'll have built <LeagueTable>, <StatCard>, <FormBadge>, and a beautiful, working standings tab.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: 1. Props for data — teams is now passed in?
react-snooker.back: Before, StandingsTab knew about TEAMS directly. Now <LeagueTable> is decoupled from where the teams come from. It's a pure rendering component: give it teams, it renders them. Anyone could reuse it.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: 2. An optional callback prop — onTeamClick??
react-snooker.back: The ? in onTeamClick?: makes the prop optional. The caller can pass it (and clicks become interactive) or not pass it (and clicks do nothing). The component handles both cases:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: IIFE inside JSX (immediately-invoked function expression)?
react-snooker.back: JSX {} expects an expression, not a sequence of statements. We have several statements (const rank = ..., const rankColor = ..., then a return). To embed those in JSX, we wrap them in an arrow function and immediately invoke it:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: A nested ternary for the colors?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const rankColor = rank === 1 ? '#FBBF24' : rank === 2 ? '#9CA3AF' : rank === 3 ? '#B45309' : '#E5E7EB'; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: display: 'inline-flex' for centering?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx display: 'inline-flex', alignItems: 'center', justifyContent: 'center', ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: The conditional coloring?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx background: team.final === 'Wu Yize' ? '#DCFCE7' : '#FEE2E2', color: team.final === 'Wu Yize' ? '#166534' : '#991B1B', ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: Lucide icons as components?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx import { TrendingUp, TrendingDown, Minus } from 'lucide-react'; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: Picking a component dynamically?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const Icon = trending === 'up' ? TrendingUp : trending === 'down' ? TrendingDown : Minus; // ... <Icon size={16} strokeWidth={3} /> ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/03-progressive-enhancement.md: CSS Grid with auto-fit and minmax?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx gridTemplateColumns: 'repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr))', ``

react-snooker.back: ``tsx const totalMatches = ROUND1MATCHES.length + ROUND2MATCHES.length + ...; const leader = teamsWithScores[0]; const avgScore = Math.round(teamsWithScores.reduce((sum, t) => sum + t.scores.total, 0) / 8); ``

react-snooker.back: ``tsx import { Trophy, Calendar, Target } from 'lucide-react'; ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-03-first-component/README.md: 'Now we write React. From a 15-line hello-world component to ...'
react-snooker.back: Now we write React. From a 15-line hello-world component to a fully styled standings table with sorted rows, medal-color rank badges, per-round columns, and a form indicator.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: 'Goal: completely demystify useState. By the end of this less...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: completely demystify useState. By the end of this lesson you can answer "what does useState do?" in a way that gets you hired, and you can predict exactly what happens to your component when state changes.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: 'Senior rule: if a piece of data can be derived from existing...'
react-snooker.back: Senior rule: if a piece of data can be derived from existing state or props, don't put it in state. Compute it on every render. Storing it duplicates the source of truth and creates synchronization bugs. State is for the minimum set of changeable values.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: The mental loop?
react-snooker.back: `` 1. React calls your function (render). 2. Your function returns JSX. 3. React updates the DOM to match the JSX. 4. User does something (clicks, types). 5. Event handler fires; calls setSomething(...). 6. React queues a re-render. 7. Go to 1. ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Rule 1: Call hooks at the top level. Never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions?
react-snooker.back: ```tsx // BROKEN function Foo({ show }) { if (show) { const [x, setX] = useState(0); // ❌ conditional hook } }

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Rule 2: Call hooks only from React function components or other hooks?
react-snooker.back: You can call useState inside a function component (function Foo()) or inside a custom hook (function useFoo()). Not inside a regular utility function. The reason is the same — React's per-component tracker only runs during component rendering.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/01-useState-mental-model.md: Hooks are detectable by name?
react-snooker.back: The convention: hooks start with use. useState, useEffect, useMemo, useReducer, useRef, useContext, etc. Custom hooks must also start with use — that's how the linter knows to enforce the rules on them.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: 'Goal: build the real <SnookerFantasyLeague> orchestrator — h...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: build the real <SnookerFantasyLeague> orchestrator — hero header, sticky tab bar, five tab buttons, conditional tab rendering, and the state that ties them together. By the end of this lesson, the app feels like an app.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: 'Senior interview answer:* "Context is great for theme/auth/l...'
react-snooker.back: Senior interview answer:* "Context is great for theme/auth/locale. For application data, I default to prop drilling because it's explicit and Cmd+F-able. If drilling becomes painful, I'd reach for Zustand or Jotai before Context.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Line 1: 'use client'?
react-snooker.back: Required because we use useState. Without it, Next.js would try to render this as a server component and crash on the first useState call.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Lines 3–13: imports?
react-snooker.back: Standard imports. Five tab components, five icons, the data and helpers. Order matters slightly: third-party (react, lucide-react), then internal ( then local (./tabs/...). Keeping that order makes the imports readable at a glance.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Lines 15: a string-union type for tab IDs?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx type TabId = 'standings' | 'matches' | 'predictions' | 'players' | 'analytics'; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Lines 18–19: the two state cells?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = useState<TabId>('standings'); const [selectedTeam, setSelectedTeam] = useState<TeamWithScores | null>(null); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Lines 21–24: the memoized derived data?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const teamsWithScores = useMemo<TeamWithScores[]>(() => { return TEAMS.map(t => ({ ...t, scores: calculateTeamScores(t) })) .sort((a, b) => b.scores.total - a.scores.total); }, []); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: Lines 26–32: the tabs configuration?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const tabs: { id: TabId; label: string; icon: typeof Trophy }[] = [ { id: 'standings', label: 'Standings', icon: Trophy }, { id: 'matches', label: 'Matches', icon: Calendar }, // ... ]; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/02-the-orchestrator-pattern.md: The && short-circuit?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx {activeTab === 'standings' && <StandingsTab ... />} ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: 'Goal: understand useMemo deeply enough that you know not jus...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: understand useMemo deeply enough that you know not just how to use it, but when not to. By the end of this lesson, you can defend every memoization decision in this codebase.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: 'Interview tip:* "useState vs useReducer?" Answer: "useState ...'
react-snooker.back: Interview tip:* "useState vs useReducer?" Answer: "useState for independent values. useReducer when state transitions become complex enough that an explicit action type makes the code clearer. They're both stored as state — useReducer is just a different ergonomic interface.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: 1. The computation is cheap?
react-snooker.back: ```tsx // ❌ Pointless — addition is faster than the memoization machinery const total = useMemo(() => count + 1, [count]);

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: 2. The result isn't being passed to a child or used in another hook's deps?
react-snooker.back:
```tsx // ❌ Pointless — totalGames is only used in this render function Component() { const games = useState(...)[0]; const totalGames = useMemo(() => games.length, [games]); // why? return
{totalGames}
; }

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: 3. The dependencies change every render anyway?
react-snooker.back: ```tsx // ❌ The dep array contains a new object every render — useMemo never hits its cache const config = useMemo(() => ({ apiKey, timeout: 5000 }), [{ apiKey, timeout: 5000 }]);

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: The senior rule?
react-snooker.back: Memoize when the result is referentially unstable AND that instability causes waste downstream. In other words: when the result is passed to a child component, used in another hook's deps, or put in Context. Otherwise leave it alone.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: Missing a dependency?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx // BUG — depends on prefix but doesn't list it const greeting = useMemo(() => ${prefix} ${name}, [name]); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: Including a dependency that breaks memoization?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx // BUG — config is recreated every render, so this never hits the cache function Component({ config }) { const result = useMemo(() => expensiveCalc(config), [config]); } ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/03-useMemo-and-derived-data.md: The right way?
react-snooker.back: The eslint rule is your safety net. Fix what it tells you. Trust ESLint over your gut for hooks-related rules — they encode subtle React behavior that's easy to miss.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-04-state-and-hooks/README.md: 'State is what makes a static page interactive. This chapter ...'
react-snooker.back: State is what makes a static page interactive. This chapter introduces useState, useMemo, the orchestrator pattern, and the most-asked React interview concept of all: lifting state up.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: 'Goal: develop the senior judgement of when JSX deserves its ...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: develop the senior judgement of when JSX deserves its own component file. Two extremes are wrong; the middle is craft.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: 'Rule of three. Some authors say: extract on the third use. T...'
react-snooker.back: Rule of three. Some authors say: extract on the third use. The first time you write something, write it. The second time, copy-paste and feel a small pang of guilt. The third time, extract. By then you understand the abstraction well enough to design it right.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Test 1: The copy-paste test?
react-snooker.back: If you're about to copy a chunk of JSX with minor variations, extract first.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Test 2: The "what vs how" test?
react-snooker.back: If a chunk of JSX is conceptually about one specific thing and the rest of the component is about something else, the chunk should be its own component.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Test 3: The reusability test?
react-snooker.back: If a chunk of JSX will be used in 2+ places (now or soon), it deserves its own component.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Signal 1: The component takes too many props?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx // Smell — 8 props is too many <TeamRow rank={i + 1} name={team.name} icon={team.icon} total={team.scores.total} r1={team.scores.r1} r2={team.scores.r2} finalPick={team.final} isLeader={i === 0} /> ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Signal 2: The component has logic that depends on its parent's context?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx function TeamRow({ team, parentTabState, parentSelectedTeam, parentSetters }) { // ... } ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/01-when-to-split-components.md: Signal 3: Extraction makes the parent harder to read?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx <TeamSection> <TeamSectionHeader> <TeamSectionHeaderTitle>{title}</TeamSectionHeaderTitle> </TeamSectionHeader> <TeamSectionBody>...</TeamSectionBody> </TeamSection> ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: 'Goal: stop being scared of prop drilling. By the end of this...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: stop being scared of prop drilling. By the end of this lesson you can defend prop drilling in interviews, recognize when Context is genuinely the right tool, and know why most internet advice on this topic is overcooked.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: 'Imagine you're inserting a new component between an ancestor...'
react-snooker.back: Imagine you're inserting a new component between an ancestor and a descendant. Do you have to add a prop to your new component just to thread something through that it doesn't use?

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: 'My app's teams array is being prop-drilled three levels deep...'
react-snooker.back: My app's teams array is being prop-drilled three levels deep: orchestrator → tab → detail → match-analysis. A teammate suggests using Context to avoid drilling. Should I? Lay out the trade-offs honestly. Default to NOT using Context unless drilling is causing real pain.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: 1. Theme?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const ThemeContext = createContext<'light' | 'dark'>('light'); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: 2. Authenticated user?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const UserContext = createContext<User | null>(null); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: What these have in common?
react-snooker.back: - Used by many components scattered across the tree. - The data is stable (it doesn't change every interaction). - The consumers are disparate — they don't share a parent above the orchestrator.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: Redux?
react-snooker.back: The classic. Boilerplate-heavy. Modern Redux Toolkit (RTK) makes it bearable, but for new projects in 2026, you reach for it only when you genuinely need:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: Default value null + assertion in the hook?
react-snooker.back: ```tsx const ThemeContext = createContext<ThemeContextValue | null>(null);

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/02-prop-drilling-vs-context.md: Style helper functions?
react-snooker.back: ``ts // lib/constants.ts export function tabStyle(active: boolean): React.CSSProperties { ... } ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/03-building-player-detail.md: 'Goal: study a real, deeply nested feature view — the Players...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: study a real, deeply nested feature view — the Players tab and its <PlayerDetail> drill-down — to see composition, prop drilling, conditional rendering, and back-and-forth navigation in action.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/03-building-player-detail.md: Local state for the detail view?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx const [selectedPlayer, setSelectedPlayer] = useState<string | null>(null); ``

react-snooker.back: ``tsx useState<string | null>(null); // ... onSelectPlayer={setSelectedPlayer} // passes a string (player name) ``

``tsx if (selectedPlayer) { return ; } return
{/ grid /}
; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/03-building-player-detail.md: A button, not a div?
react-snooker.back: The card is a <button>. Why? Accessibility. Screen readers announce buttons differently from divs; keyboard users can Tab to a button and Enter on it. A <div onClick> traps both.

react-snooker.back: ``tsx onMouseOver={(e) => { e.currentTarget.style.transform = 'translateY(-3px)'; }} onMouseOut={(e) => { e.currentTarget.style.transform = 'none'; }} ``

react-snooker.back: This is the data shape the inner component (<MatchPickAnalysis>) needs:

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-05-composition/README.md: 'A 200-line component is a smell. So is splitting too early. ...'
react-snooker.back: A 200-line component is a smell. So is splitting too early. This chapter teaches you the senior judgement of when to extract a component, when to leave one alone, and how to build deeply nested feature views without losing the plot.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: 'Goal: understand the mental model behind Recharts so the act...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: understand the mental model behind Recharts so the actual API feels predictable. By the end of this lesson, you can read any Recharts code in the wild and describe what it draws without running it.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: <CartesianGrid> — the dotted/dashed grid?
react-snooker.back: Draws faint lines across the chart for visual reference. The strokeDasharray="3 3" says "3px line, 3px gap" — i.e. dotted/dashed.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: <XAxis> — the X-axis?
react-snooker.back: The dataKey="team" is the categorical key — Recharts looks at each row's team field to label the axis. If you forget dataKey, Recharts uses array indices (0, 1, 2, ...), which is rarely what you want.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: <YAxis> — the Y-axis?
react-snooker.back: Recharts auto-computes the domain (min/max) from your data. If you want to force it: domain={[0, 100]}. Useful for percentages where 100 is the natural max.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: <Tooltip> — the hover tooltip?
react-snooker.back: When the user hovers a bar, this floating box shows the values. Customizable via <Tooltip contentStyle={{...}}> or, for full control, a custom content={MyCustomTooltip} render prop.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/01-recharts-fundamentals.md: <Legend> — the color key below?
react-snooker.back: Shows which color = which series. Generated automatically from <Bar name="Round 1"> props.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: 'Goal: master the three transforms that produce 90% of the ch...'
react-snooker.back: Goal: master the three transforms that produce 90% of the charts you'll ever build — .map(), .filter(), .reduce() — applied to real data. Plus per-bar color overrides with <Cell>.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: Abbreviating long team names?
react-snooker.back: ``ts team: t.name.length > 12 ? t.name.split(' ').map(w => w[0]).join('') : t.name, ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: The numeric fields are the bar heights?
react-snooker.back: ``ts R1: t.scores.r1, R2: t.scores.r2, QF: t.scores.qf, SF: t.scores.sf, Final: t.scores.f, ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: The chart that consumes it?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx <BarChart data={r1Data}> <Bar dataKey="R1" stackId="a" fill="#0F5132" name="Round 1" /> <Bar dataKey="R2" stackId="a" fill="#DC2626" name="Round 2" /> <Bar dataKey="QF" stackId="a" fill="#FBBF24" name="Quarter-Finals" /> <Bar dataKey="SF" stackId="a" fill="#7C3AED" name="Semi-Finals" /> <Bar dataKey="Final" stackId="a" fill="#9F1239" name="Final" radius={[4, 4, 0, 0]} /> </BarChart> ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: .filter() to count hits?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const r1Hits = t.scores.r1Details.filter(d => d.correct).length; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: The horizontal-bar chart that consumes it?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx <BarChart data={accuracyData} layout="vertical"> <XAxis type="number" domain={[0, 100]} unit="%" /> <YAxis dataKey="team" type="category" /> <Bar dataKey="R1" fill="#16A34A" /> <Bar dataKey="R2" fill="#F59E0B" /> <Bar dataKey="QF" fill="#0EA5E9" /> <Bar dataKey="SF" fill="#7C3AED" /> <Bar dataKey="F" fill="#9F1239" /> </BarChart> ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: Counting with .forEach() and a dictionary?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const finalPickCount: Record<string, number> = {}; teams.forEach(t => { if (t.final) finalPickCount[t.final] = (finalPickCount[t.final] || 0) + 1; }); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: What a <Cell> does?
react-snooker.back: A <Cell> overrides the <Bar>'s default fill for one specific row. The map produces one <Cell> per data row, in order. Recharts uses cell N for the N-th bar.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: Why this exists?
react-snooker.back: Without <Cell>, every bar in a <Bar> gets the same fill. You'd have to render multiple <Bar>s with different dataKeys to get different colors — but that's a different chart structurally.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/02-reshaping-data.md: The label prop on <Bar>?
react-snooker.back: ``tsx label={{ position: 'top', fontSize: 16, fontWeight: 800, fill: '#1F2937' }} ``

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 'Some state belongs to a single component. This lesson is abo...'
react-snooker.back: Some state belongs to a single component. This lesson is about a chart that owns its own UI state — and about pivoting data so the match (not the team) becomes the unit of analysis.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 'Interview answer: "I'd use a discriminated union if the cons...'
react-snooker.back: Interview answer: "I'd use a discriminated union if the consumer of the type does a lot of branching on finished. I went with optional fields here because the chart is the only consumer, and the branching happens once."

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 'Lifting state up is a real React principle, but the rule is:...'
react-snooker.back: Lifting state up is a real React principle, but the rule is: lift state only when something else needs it. Nothing outside this chart cares which tab is open. So it stays here.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 'Interview answer: "I extract to lib/ when (a) the function i...'
react-snooker.back: Interview answer: "I extract to lib/ when (a) the function is pure and (b) more than one component needs it. This one fails (b) for now, so I keep it local."

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 'Interview answer: "I use .filter().length when readability b...'
react-snooker.back: Interview answer: "I use .filter().length when readability beats one extra pass over the array. For arrays of millions of elements, .reduce() is faster because it avoids the intermediate array. For 8 teams? It doesn't matter."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: Why these types??
react-snooker.back: - AgreementDatum describes one bar on the chart. - AgreementRound describes one tab — Round 1, Round 2, etc. — bundling the round's name, subtitle, and the array of bars.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: Why export interface??
react-snooker.back: So that other files can import AgreementDatum and use it as the return type of helper functions. Concretely, AnalyticsTab.tsx will do:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: Why a simple button row instead of a <select> or radio??
react-snooker.back: Because we have a small, fixed set of options (5 rounds) and we want each option to be a prominent target. Buttons in a row are touch-friendly, glanceable, and don't hide options behind a click.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: Why one onClick={() => setActive(r.id)} per button??
react-snooker.back: Each button needs its own handler that knows its id. The arrow function creates a closure that captures r.id. This is standard React.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 8.2. What is pickKey doing??
react-snooker.back: pickKey is a string parameter that tells the function which array on the team to read: t.r1, t.r2, t.qf, t.sf, or — special-cased — t.final.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 8.3. Why the special case for 'final'??
react-snooker.back: Because the team type has t.final: string (a single player name), not t.final: string[] (an array of picks). So:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 8.4. The label trick?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const matchLabel = ${m.p1.split(' ').slice(-1)[0]} v ${m.p2.split(' ').slice(-1)[0]}; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 8.5. The vote count?
react-snooker.back: ``ts const correct = teams.filter((t) => / pick equals winner /).length; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 8.6. The implicit invariant?
react-snooker.back: We have 8 fantasy teams. Every team picks for every match. So correct + wrong = 8. Always.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: When to keep state local?
react-snooker.back: - Only this component reads it. - Only this component writes it. - The state doesn't need to survive remounting. - The state doesn't need to be shared with the URL or persistence.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: When to lift state up?
react-snooker.back: - A sibling component needs to read it. - A parent component needs to react to it. - The state should persist (e.g., URL param, localStorage). - Two pieces of state must stay in sync.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: The diff is roughly:?
react-snooker.back: | Concern | Local state | Lifted state | | ------- | ----------- | ------------ | | Component count needing it | 1 | 2+ | | Persistence required | No | Sometimes | | URL reflects it | No | Sometimes | | Resets on remount | OK | Often not OK | | Test isolation | Easier | Harder |

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/03-pivots-and-internal-state.md: 11.3. Lifting active into the orchestrator "just in case"?
react-snooker.back: A team I worked with did this once. Every chart's "active tab" lived in a top-level reducer. Why? "In case we want to share active tabs across charts."

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-06-data-visualization/README.md: 'Charts in React are about data shape, not chart libraries. T...'
react-snooker.back: Charts in React are about data shape, not chart libraries. This chapter teaches you Recharts — but more importantly, it teaches you the transform-then-render pattern that applies to every charting library you'll ever touch.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 'The features work. Now make the app robust. This lesson is t...'
react-snooker.back: The features work. Now make the app robust. This lesson is the bag of small, high-leverage improvements that separate "demo-quality" from "ship-quality."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.1. Use semantic HTML, not <div> soup?
react-snooker.back: A <button> is not a <div onClick={...}>. The browser gives you keyboard support, focus rings, and screen-reader announcements for free when you use the right element.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.2. Every image must have alt text?
react-snooker.back: We don't use heavily — most icons come from lucide-react (which renders SVG with aria-hidden by default). But if you ever add , write alt="Trophy" next to it. Decorative images get alt="" (empty string, not missing).

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.3. Tab interfaces need role="tablist", role="tab", role="tabpanel"?
react-snooker.back: Open components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx. Find the tab nav. Right now it looks like:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.4. Every interactive element must be keyboard-reachable?
react-snooker.back: Try this: open your app in a browser and press Tab repeatedly. Can you get to every button? Every link? Every team row in the standings?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 2.5. Color contrast: text must be readable?
react-snooker.back: Open Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse → Accessibility audit. It will flag any text whose contrast ratio drops below 4.5:1 (3:1 for large text).

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 3.1. The class-component reality?
react-snooker.back: Error boundaries are one of the two places React still requires a class component (the other is the componentDidCatch lifecycle that error boundaries themselves use). There is no hook for this. Yet.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 3.3. The interview question this answers?
react-snooker.back: > Q: "How do you handle errors in a React app?" > > A: "Three layers. (1) Error boundaries around any component subtree that could crash, with a friendly fallback UI and a logger that ships the error to Sentry. (2) Try/catch around any async work that could reject — fetch failures, JSON parse errors. (3) Defensive null checks in the render path for any data that might be missing. Error boundaries don't catch errors in event handlers or async code, so the three layers complement each other."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 4.1. When does this fire??
react-snooker.back: Whenever a route's server component is doing async work (await, dynamic imports). Right now our app is fully static — all data is in lib/. So loading.tsx will rarely fire in this app.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 4.2. Why aria-live="polite"??
react-snooker.back: Screen readers announce the loading state to the user, but in a non-disruptive way. aria-live="assertive" interrupts whatever the user is hearing — too aggressive for a spinner.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 5.1. What to look at?
react-snooker.back: - Size — the JS for just this route's code. - First Load JS — the JS the browser downloads when it first hits this route, including shared chunks. - Shared chunks — code split out so that navigating between routes doesn't re-download it.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 5.2. The numbers to worry about?
react-snooker.back: For a public-facing app, First Load JS under 200 KB is excellent. Under 500 KB is fine. Over 1 MB is a red flag.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 6.1. The three highest-leverage fixes?
react-snooker.back: Almost every React app has the same three issues on the first Lighthouse run:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 7.1. The useMemo you already wrote?
react-snooker.back: Remember useMemo from Chapter 4? You used it for teamsWithScores. That's the kind of thing that helps interaction performance. Without it, every re-render recomputes scores for every team. With it, that work is done once.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 7.2. React.memo for expensive children?
react-snooker.back: If you ever have a chart that re-renders on every parent state change, wrap its export:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 7.3. The React DevTools Profiler?
react-snooker.back: Install React DevTools (browser extension). Open the Profiler tab. Click record. Click around the app. Stop recording. You'll see a flame graph of what re-rendered and how long it took.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 8.1. Guard against missing data?
react-snooker.back:
``tsx {team.scores ? :
No scores yet.
} ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/01-production-polish.md: 8.2. Empty states?
react-snooker.back: When an array is empty, render an empty state, not a blank gap:

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'Code that isn't deployed isn't real, and code that isn't tes...'
react-snooker.back: Code that isn't deployed isn't real, and code that isn't tested isn't trusted. This lesson teaches you to ship the app to the public internet and to write the small set of tests that catch the bugs that actually happen.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'Per the project rules, deployment goes through GitHub → Verc...'
react-snooker.back: Per the project rules, deployment goes through GitHub → Vercel automatically. We are not using vercel deploy from the CLI. We are not using Netlify. We push to GitHub; Vercel pulls from GitHub.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, ...'
react-snooker.back: The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you. — Kent C. Dodds

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 'A small project deserves a small test suite. Don't aim for 1...'
react-snooker.back: A small project deserves a small test suite. Don't aim for 100% coverage; aim for the five tests that catch the bugs that actually happen.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 4.1. Local .env.local?
react-snooker.back: Create .env.local in the project root (it's already gitignored):

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: 4.2. Vercel environment variables?
react-snooker.back: In the Vercel dashboard: Project → Settings → Environment Variables. Add the same keys, but with the production values. Choose which environments they apply to: Production, Preview, Development.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: What's worth noting?
react-snooker.back: - We test the pure functions because they're cheap and high-confidence. Pure-function tests are the highest ROI tests in any codebase. - We test the shape of the return value, not specific scores from real data. Tying tests to real-world results is brittle — when the tournament resets next year, your tests break for the wrong reason. - We test boundary conditions: empty arrays, undefined winners. Bugs hide in the boundaries.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: What this test catches?
react-snooker.back: - Hydration errors — if the server-rendered HTML doesn't match the client tree, Playwright would see a console error. - Routing bugs — if a tab click stops working, the test fails. - Missing accessibility roles — getByRole('tab') only finds elements with the right ARIA. Half of writing E2E tests is being forced to add proper a11y, which is a feature.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: Why include npm run build??
react-snooker.back: Because a passing test suite isn't worth much if the production build is broken. The build is itself a giant smoke test — it type-checks, bundles, and lints everything.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/02-deployment-and-testing.md: Why not run E2E in CI yet??
react-snooker.back: Playwright in CI requires a Linux runner with browsers preinstalled, plus more orchestration. It's worth doing eventually. For a personal portfolio project, run E2E locally before pushing big changes.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Building the app got you in the room. Talking about it gets ...'
react-snooker.back: Building the app got you in the room. Talking about it gets you the offer. This is the lesson that turns 1,654 lines of code into a 90-second story.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DESCRIBE THE DA...'
react-snooker.back: I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DESCRIBE THE DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [DESCRIBE 3-5 USER ACTIONS]. Don't write code yet — just confirm you understand the data and the actions, and propose a 5-tab UI. Push back on anything that's ambiguous.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Define TypeScript interfaces for [the entities]. Be strict —...'
react-snooker.back: Define TypeScript interfaces for [the entities]. Be strict — no any, no implicit optional fields. Make every invariant explicit with a comment. If two interfaces share fields, use composition rather than inheritance.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTP...'
react-snooker.back: Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects, no React, no state. The functions should be testable in isolation. Add JSDoc comments explaining each one.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Render [the simplest possible thing] using my types from lib...'
react-snooker.back: Render [the simplest possible thing] using my types from lib/types.ts. Use a functional component with explicit props. No useState. No useMemo. Just JSX over the data. Use inline styles for now.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT]. Don't redesign an...'
react-snooker.back: Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT]. Don't redesign anything. Don't refactor unrelated code. If the change touches more than three files, stop and tell me which files, before changing them.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Refactor [SINGLE-COMPONENT FILE] into an orchestrator patter...'
react-snooker.back: Refactor [SINGLE-COMPONENT FILE] into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns [STATE PIECES] with useState. Tab components receive props and a callback. Use useMemo for [DERIVED VALUE]. Keep all styling identical.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Extract a [COMPONENT NAME] from [PARENT FILE]. Move only the...'
react-snooker.back: Extract a [COMPONENT NAME] from [PARENT FILE]. Move only the JSX that has a single responsibility — [DESCRIBE]. Pass props for [INPUTS]. Don't introduce Context. Don't change behavior.
