react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 'Re-read course/README.md any time you want a refresher on th...'
react-snooker.back: Re-read course/README.md any time you want a refresher on the chapter map. The original single-file walkthrough lives at course/SNOOKERFANTASYLEAGUE_COURSE.md for one-stop reading.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 1.3. The variant for non-technical interviewers?
react-snooker.back: If you're talking to a recruiter or a non-engineer hiring manager:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.1. "Walk me through your folder structure."?
react-snooker.back: > "Top-level: app/ for routes, components/ for UI, lib/ for data and pure logic, course/ for documentation. Inside components/, I split by role, not by type: a tabs/ folder for top-level tab components, a standings/ folder for everything that's only used inside the standings tab, and so on. The advantage is that when I want to delete the analytics tab, I delete one folder and don't have to chase down stragglers in a generic widgets/ directory."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.2. "How does state management work in your app?"?
react-snooker.back: > "I deliberately don't use Redux or Zustand. The app has two pieces of shared state — the active tab and the selected team — and both live in the top-level SnookerFantasyLeague component as plain useState. Tab-local state, like which round is selected inside the matches tab, lives inside that tab. The rule I follow: lift state only when something else needs it. State libraries are tools for the day a useState tree becomes painful — and that day hasn't come for an 8-team app."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.3. "How do you decide when to extract a component?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Two triggers. First, when I copy-paste the same JSX twice — the second time I write a function. Second, when a piece of JSX has a name: 'this is a stat card,' 'this is a path step.' Naming forces extraction. I avoid premature extraction because over-componentized React is just as painful as under-componentized — you end up chasing props through ten files instead of two."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.4. "How do you handle data fetching?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Right now this app's data is hardcoded — eight teams, ~60 matches, all in lib/. So technically there's no fetching. The way I'd add it: convert app/page.tsx to an async server component, await fetch from a sports-results API at request time, and pass the data down. The pure scoring functions in lib/scoring.ts would not change at all. That's the value of separating data from logic from UI."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.5. "What's the trade-off between server and client components?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Server components run only on the server. They can read environment variables and talk to databases, but they can't use useState or event handlers. Client components run in the browser. They have hooks and interactivity, but they bloat the JS bundle. The default in App Router is server, and I push 'use client' down to leaves — only the orchestrator and any tab that uses useState get that directive. The static parts above stay server-rendered."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.6. "How do you optimize performance in React?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Three layers. First, the render layer — useMemo for derived data, React.memo for expensive components, but only after profiling says I need them. Second, the bundle layer — next/dynamic for code-splitting heavy tabs like analytics with Recharts. Third, the paint layer — loading.tsx for instant skeletons during transitions. I never optimize before measuring; the React DevTools Profiler is my first stop."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.7. "How do you handle errors?"?
react-snooker.back: > "An error boundary at the root catches anything thrown during render and shows a friendly fallback. Async errors don't bubble through error boundaries, so I wrap fetches in try/catch and surface the message in the UI. In production I'd ship componentDidCatch output to Sentry. And I lean hard on TypeScript to make 'this object might be undefined' a compile-time error rather than a runtime one."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.8. "What's your testing strategy?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Five tests for this app. Three Vitest unit tests on the pure scoring functions — those are the highest ROI tests in the codebase. One React Testing Library integration test that renders the standings tab and asserts every team name is in the DOM. One Playwright E2E that confirms tab switching works. Plus the full TypeScript build, which is itself a giant static test. I aim for confidence, not coverage percentage."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.9. "How would you scale this to 1,000 teams?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Three things change. First, the data moves out of lib/ into a database — Postgres for relational picks-vs-matches, with a real foreign key instead of array-index correspondence. Second, the orchestrator's useMemo over all teams becomes a paginated server-side query — you don't render 1,000 rows in a table. Third, the Recharts visualizations either get aggregated server-side or switch to a canvas-based library because SVG can't draw 1,000 bars. None of those changes touch the UI layer's component shape — that's a deliberate result of the data/logic/UI separation."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 2.10. "What would you do differently?"?
react-snooker.back: This is the one that trips most candidates. They say "nothing." Wrong answer. The right answer:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.1. The project-shaping prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DESCRIBE THE DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [DESCRIBE 3-5 USER ACTIONS]. Don't write code yet — just confirm you understand the data and the actions, and propose a 5-tab UI. Push back on anything that's ambiguous."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.2. The data-modeling prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Define TypeScript interfaces for [the entities]. Be strict — no any, no implicit optional fields. Make every invariant explicit with a comment. If two interfaces share fields, use composition rather than inheritance."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.3. The pure-logic prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects, no React, no state. The functions should be testable in isolation. Add JSDoc comments explaining each one."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.4. The first-component prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Render [the simplest possible thing] using my types from lib/types.ts. Use a functional component with explicit props. No useState. No useMemo. Just JSX over the data. Use inline styles for now."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.5. The progressive-enhancement prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT]. Don't redesign anything. Don't refactor unrelated code. If the change touches more than three files, stop and tell me which files, before changing them."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.6. The state-and-tabs prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Refactor [SINGLE-COMPONENT FILE] into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns [STATE PIECES] with useState. Tab components receive props and a callback. Use useMemo for [DERIVED VALUE]. Keep all styling identical."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.7. The composition prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Extract a [COMPONENT NAME] from [PARENT FILE]. Move only the JSX that has a single responsibility — [DESCRIBE]. Pass props for [INPUTS]. Don't introduce Context. Don't change behavior."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.8. The charting prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Generate a Recharts [CHART TYPE] from this data shape: [PASTE A SAMPLE ROW]. Bars stacked by [KEY1] and [KEY2]. Use <ResponsiveContainer> and the <ChartCard> wrapper from components/analytics/ChartCard.tsx. Match the existing color palette."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.9. The production-polish prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Audit my [COMPONENT] for: (1) ARIA roles, (2) keyboard reachability, (3) loading and empty states, (4) defensive null checks. Add them in-place without changing the visual design."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 3.10. The testing prompt?
react-snooker.back: > "Write [3 unit + 1 integration + 1 E2E] tests. Unit tests cover [PURE FUNCTION] with [3 BOUNDARY CASES]. Integration test renders [COMPONENT] with [REAL DATA] and asserts [VISIBLE BEHAVIOR]. E2E test opens the app and [USER ACTION]. Use Vitest, RTL, Playwright."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.2. "Why didn't you use Redux / Zustand / TanStack Query?"?
react-snooker.back: > "Because the app has two pieces of shared state and zero remote data. Redux solves a problem this app doesn't have yet. TanStack Query solves a problem this app will have the day I add live API data — and that's the day I'd pull it in. Premature library adoption is a cost: extra learning, extra surface area for bugs, extra bundle size. I add libraries when local code starts to suffer, not preemptively."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.3. "How would I know if a feature you added broke something?"?
react-snooker.back: > "The CI pipeline runs lint, type-check, unit tests, integration tests, and the production build on every PR. The Playwright E2E covers the tab-switching happy path. Vercel preview deployments give a clickable URL on every PR for visual review. Beyond that, I'd want a real user-tracking and error-tracking layer in production — Sentry for crashes, PostHog or similar for usage. None of that is in the project yet because it's a personal app, but I'd add it on day one of a paid project."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 5.4. "What's the most React-specific bug you've fixed?"?
react-snooker.back: Tell a story. The structure is STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 6.1. About the codebase?
react-snooker.back: - "What's the one part of your codebase that everyone on the team agrees needs a rewrite, but nobody has time for?"

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/03-interview-narrative.md: 6.2. About the team?
react-snooker.back: - "How do you decide what's worth a unit test vs. an integration test on this team?"

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/chapter-07-mastery-and-interviews/README.md: 'You can build it. Now learn to ship it and talk about it. Th...'
react-snooker.back: You can build it. Now learn to ship it and talk about it. This chapter is the difference between a junior developer who finished a tutorial and an engineer who can stand behind their work in an interview.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/README.md: 'A structured, beginner-friendly course that teaches you Reac...'
react-snooker.back: A structured, beginner-friendly course that teaches you React from scratch by rebuilding a real, working fantasy-sports app — one component at a time, in the order a senior engineer would actually build it. Designed to take you from zero to interview-ready.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/README.md: Pace yourself?
react-snooker.back: The full course is about 35,000 words plus typing the code. Realistically:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/README.md: Don't skip Chapter 1?
react-snooker.back: Chapter 1 has zero React in it. It's about the problem and the data model. Beginners always want to skip to "the React part." Don't. Half of senior interview questions are "How would you model X?" and "What state is owned where?" — and those are answered before any JSX gets typed. Chapter 1 is where you build the muscle for that.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'A nine-lesson walk-back from a finished 1,654-line React app...'
react-snooker.back: A nine-lesson walk-back from a finished 1,654-line React app to the first commit it would have come from. Read alongside _source/SnookerFantasyLeague.jsx open in another window.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or r2, or qf, o...'
react-snooker.back: For each round, the i-th element of team.r1 (or r2, or qf, or sf) corresponds to the i-th element of ROUND1MATCHES (or ROUND2MATCHES, …).

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'State up, callbacks down. The state lives at the highest com...'
react-snooker.back: State up, callbacks down. The state lives at the highest component that needs to see all of it. The leaf components don't own state; they receive props and call back when they want a change.

react-snooker.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: 'One feature. One file (or a small set of named files). One s...'
react-snooker.back: One feature. One file (or a small set of named files). One sentence per change. Avoid technology names. Avoid superlatives. Mention specific data fields and component names. Refer to behavior, not "polish."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Imagine the pub conversation?
react-snooker.back: You and seven mates watch the World Snooker Championship every May. Sheffield, Crucible, two weeks of green-baize warfare. Someone — probably you — says: "We should make a fantasy league out of this." Everyone agrees. Pints are raised. Now what?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The 8 teams?
react-snooker.back: There are eight fantasy teams: Invincibles, Uncredibles, Hopeless, Clueless, Break Builders United, The Untouchables, Selbies, One Four Sevens. Each one is a person (or a couple) sitting at home with their bracket, picking who they think wins each match. Each team needs:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The scoring rules (3 bullets)?
react-snooker.back: - 3 points if your pick wins the match. - 1 point (a "consolation") if your pick loses — you still showed up. - null / pending if the match hasn't been played yet — neither right nor wrong, just not scored.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: What the app needs to do?
react-snooker.back: When the tournament is live, you want to be able to: see who's leading, see how each round shook out, drill into one team's picks, drill into one player's appearances, and see charts of who agreed and who didn't. That's it. No login, no database, no sharing. It's a static page; the data is edited by hand as results come in.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Vibe prompt you would have used?
react-snooker.back: > "I want to build a single-page React app for a fantasy snooker league. Eight teams pick winners across five rounds (R1: 16 picks, R2: 8, QF: 4, SF: 2, Final: 1). Scoring: 3 points if the pick wins, 1 if it loses, null while the match is unplayed. No database — the bracket and team picks are edited by hand in code. The page should have tabs for Standings, Matches, Predictions, Players, and Analytics. Don't write any code yet — just confirm you understand the problem and ask me anything that's ambiguous."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: CHECK YOURSELF?
react-snooker.back: 1. Why does scoring use null for unplayed matches instead of 0? 2. Why are there exactly 31 picks per team, no more and no less? 3. If a 9th friend wanted to join, what's the minimum data change required? (Hint: it's a one-line addition.)

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: What's PLAYER_INFO and why is it an object keyed by name??
react-snooker.back: ``jsx const PLAYER_INFO = { 'Zhao Xintong': { country: '🇨🇳', seed: 1, status: 'Defending Champion', flag: 'CHN' }, 'Judd Trump': { country: '🏴', seed: 2, status: 'World No. 1', flag: 'ENG' }, // ... 30 more }; ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The most important rule in the codebase?
react-snooker.back: Here is the invariant that the entire scoring engine depends on:

Why is PLAYERINFO keyed by name and not by seed? 3. team.final is a string but team.sf is an array. What's the smallest code change that would make them consistent, and what would it cost in readability?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Why scoring lives outside React?
react-snooker.back: The original file declares two functions before the first JSX tag:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Walking through scorePick line by line?
react-snooker.back: ``jsx function scorePick(pick, match) { if (!match || !match.winner) return null; // pending if (pick === match.winner) return 3; // correct return 1; // wrong (consolation) } ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Why correct is null, not false?
react-snooker.back: Inside calculateTeamScores you'll see this pattern repeated for every round:

Why is calculateTeamScores called inside useMemo instead of inline in the JSX? 3. Which property of the return value of calculateTeamScores do the standings columns read, and which property do the predictions matrix cells read? Why are both needed?

react-snooker.back: The orchestrator component is <SnookerFantasyLeague>. Its top three lines:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Wiring the tabs?
react-snooker.back: After the state and the memoized data, the component renders three things:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Why not put selectedTeam inside <PredictionsTab>??
react-snooker.back: Because then the <StandingsTab>'s row click couldn't reach it. The state has to live at the lowest common ancestor of every component that needs to read or write it. For selectedTeam, the readers are <PredictionsTab> and the writers are both <StandingsTab> (when you click a row) and <PredictionsTab> (when you click a team chip inside it). Their lowest common ancestor is <SnookerFantasyLeague> itself, so that's where the state lives.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: CHECK YOURSELF?

Why does setSelectedTeam get passed down to <PredictionsTab> as a prop instead of being created with its own useState inside that tab? 3. Why does <MatchesTab /> not need any props at all, while <StandingsTab> needs two?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The chain, explained?
react-snooker.back: <PlayersTab> (top of the players folder) has two jobs: render the 32-player grid, and — when a player is clicked — render <PlayerDetail> for that player. The rule "two jobs" is fine in a parent component; that's literally what an orchestrator does. The actual rendering of those two jobs is delegated:

react-snooker.back: If you find yourself writing the same JSX twice with minor tweaks, that's the signal. Inside <MatchPickAnalysis>:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Prop drilling: when it's fine?
react-snooker.back: teams gets passed from <SnookerFantasyLeague> to <PlayersTab> to <PlayerDetail> to <MatchPickAnalysis>. Three levels. That's "prop drilling" and at some point newcomers ask "shouldn't this be a Context or a Redux store?"

Why is the column wrapper inside <MatchPickAnalysis> not extracted, even though it appears twice with similar structure? 3. At what nesting depth would you reach for Context instead of prop drilling? Why isn't this app there yet?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Reshape #1: stacked-bar totals?
react-snooker.back: ``jsx const r1Data = teams.map((t) => ({ team: t.name.length > 12 ? t.name .split(' ') .map((w) => w[0]) .join('') : t.name, fullName: t.name, R1: t.scores.r1, R2: t.scores.r2, QF: t.scores.qf, SF: t.scores.sf, Final: t.scores.f, Total: t.scores.total, })); ``

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Reshape #2: per-bar colors with <Cell>?
react-snooker.back: The "Pick Distribution" chart for the Final has a special twist: each bar should be green if the player picked is Wu Yize, gray otherwise. Recharts lets you do that by mapping <Cell> children:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Reshape #3: pivot from "team picks" to "match agreement"?
react-snooker.back: The most interesting chart on the page is <LeagueAgreementChart>. The question it answers is: "For each match in this round, of the 8 teams, how many picked the actual winner?" That's a pivot — the source data is per-team-per-round, but the chart needs per-match-per-round.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Internal tab state inside a chart?
react-snooker.back: <LeagueAgreementChart> is the only chart that has its own useState:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: CHECK YOURSELF?
react-snooker.back: 1. If you remove the stackId="a" prop from every <Bar> in the points-breakdown chart, what changes visually? 2. Why does <LeagueAgreementChart> keep its own useState instead of putting active in the top-level <SnookerFantasyLeague>? 3. The agreement-data builder has two return shapes (finished vs pending). What does the chart code use to decide which <Bar> series to render — and what would happen if a single round had a mix of finished and unfinished matches?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 1 — "Just give me the standings"?
react-snooker.back: Pre-tournament. R1 hasn't started. You have the bracket. You have eight friends' picks. You want a single screen that shows team totals.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 2 — "I want to see the matches themselves"?
react-snooker.back: Round 1 is half-finished. People want to know which matches are done and what the scores were.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 3 — "I want to compare predictions side by side"?
react-snooker.back: End of Round 1. The standings tab tells you the total, but doesn't show whose pick was wrong. You add a third screen.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 4 — "Round 2 happened, the bracket shrunk"?
react-snooker.back: Round 1 is done. Round 2 results trickle in. You add ROUND2_MATCHES and a r2 array on every team. The whole calculateTeamScores function gets duplicated for R2:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 5 — "Show me a player's tournament journey"?
react-snooker.back: Mid-tournament, QF in flight. The Standings + Matches + Predictions trio is the league-level view. But you want to be able to ask, "How is Wu Yize doing? Which teams backed him? Did they get points?" That's the Players tab.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 6 — "I want pretty charts"?
react-snooker.back: Late tournament. With most matches in, you want trend visuals — points by round, accuracy by round, agreement on each match.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Milestone 7 — "Wu Yize won. Crown him."?
react-snooker.back: Tournament ends. The Final result is in: Wu Yize 18, Murphy 17. The codebase needs to acknowledge that the champion is decided and the league is done.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Putting the timeline together?
react-snooker.back: If you read the file with this timeline in mind, you can almost see the seams between sessions. The five *Details arrays returned by calculateTeamScores each got added in their own session. The <RoundButton> and <PicksList> components were added at slightly different times — the round button has a status prop with literal English ('FINISHED', '🏆 WU WINS 18-17'), which is unmistakably end-of-tournament wording, while <PicksList> is generic (it would render the same on day 1). The newer code is more specific to "the tournament is over;" the older code is more generic to "any tournament."

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Vibe prompt you would have used (the meta-pattern)?
react-snooker.back: There isn't a single prompt for the whole evolution — that's the point. There's one prompt per milestone, and each prompt is shaped like an addition, not a redesign:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: CHECK YOURSELF?
react-snooker.back: 1. The Final Pick chip's red/blue coloring is a Milestone 7 artifact. What earlier file evidence (in any other component) tells you the codebase wasn't always tournament-end-aware? 2. Why is each round's logic copy-pasted (one block for r1, one for r2, …) instead of looped over ['r1', 'r2', 'qf', 'sf', 'final']? What would looping cost in readability for a vibe coder reading this file? 3. The Players tab is by far the biggest single milestone in the timeline. Why is that a smell only if you have to ship by Friday — and why is it fine for this project?

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The seven shapes of a good vibe prompt?
react-snooker.back: After two weeks of building this app, your prompt library should look like this:

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: The prompt-tree of this app?
react-snooker.back: If you imagine the file as a tree of prompts, the trunk is Milestone 1's "Give me the standings" prompt. Every subsequent prompt is a branch off an existing branch — never the trunk. You never re-prompt the standings table from scratch. You always say "in the standings, change X." That discipline is what keeps a vibe-coded app from becoming a churning rewrite machine.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: Final word?
react-snooker.back: The thing this course was reverse-engineering wasn't just a 1,654-line file. It was the prompting strategy that grew that file without rewrites. If you internalize Lesson 4's "state up, callbacks down," Lesson 5's "extract when you copy-paste twice," Lesson 6's "data shape, not chart library," and Lesson 8's "Add X to Y showing Z," you can build the next app — pickleball ladder, World Cup pool, whatever — in maybe a third of the prompts.

react-snooker.front: In react-snooker/SNOOKER_FANTASY_LEAGUE_COURSE.md: CHECK YOURSELF?
react-snooker.back: 1. Look back at any vibe prompt you've written this month. Which of the seven shapes does it match? If none, what's missing — a target component name? a behavior? a data field? 2. Why is "Use Redux for state" almost always the wrong prompt for an app of this size, and what is the right prompt to write when you feel that itch? 3. The seven prompt shapes in this lesson all share one structural feature. What is it? (Hint: count how many of them name a specific component, file, or constant by name.)

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'If your interviewer asks "How do you use AI in your work?" a...'
ai-interview.back: If your interviewer asks "How do you use AI in your work?" and you say "I use Copilot for autocomplete," you've already lost the room. This lesson teaches you to answer that question like a senior engineer.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-fi...'
ai-interview.back: I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-file refactors and data-shaping. For one-off questions or unfamiliar APIs I'll drop into a G2/G3 chat. I haven't moved fully to G5 autonomous agents yet because I want to review every diff before it lands.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You rev...'
ai-interview.back: You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You review and accept.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'You don't know what you want. You explore options with the A...'
ai-interview.back: You don't know what you want. You explore options with the AI as a thinking partner.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI i...'
ai-interview.back: The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI iterate until it's right.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 'I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when ...'
ai-interview.back: I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when I know exactly what I want, exploration when I'm trying to choose between approaches, and iteration when I'm refining something that already exists. Most of my real work is iteration.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: How to drop this casually in an interview?
ai-interview.back: > "I'm primarily on G4 — agentic editing. I use it for multi-file refactors and data-shaping. For one-off questions or unfamiliar APIs I'll drop into a G2/G3 chat. I haven't moved fully to G5 autonomous agents yet because I want to review every diff before it lands."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.1. Generation workflow?
ai-interview.back: > You know what you want. You ask. The AI produces it. You review and accept.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.2. Exploration workflow?
ai-interview.back: > You don't know what you want. You explore options with the AI as a thinking partner.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 2.3. Iteration workflow?
ai-interview.back: > The AI generated something. It's 80% there. You and the AI iterate until it's right.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: How to drop this in an interview?
ai-interview.back: > "I think of AI-assisted work in three modes: generation when I know exactly what I want, exploration when I'm trying to choose between approaches, and iteration when I'm refining something that already exists. Most of my real work is iteration."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.1. What "vibe coding" means at the good end?
ai-interview.back: - Iterating quickly with AI assistance. - Trusting your taste about whether the output looks right. - Skipping ceremony when ceremony doesn't earn its keep. - Letting the LLM type so you can think.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.2. What "vibe coding" means at the bad end?
ai-interview.back: - Shipping code you can't read. - "It works" being the only test. - Not understanding the abstractions the AI introduced. - Copying solutions to problems you couldn't articulate.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 3.3. How interviewers use the phrase?
ai-interview.back: Interviewers asking "Are you a vibe coder?" are usually asking one of three different questions:

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.1. Pattern-rich, low-novelty work?
ai-interview.back: - Boilerplate (form scaffolds, CRUD endpoints, type definitions). - Conversions (JSON → TS interfaces, CSS → Tailwind, Python → Go). - Test scaffolding (writing the next test that follows the existing pattern). - Documentation (turning code into prose, generating JSDoc/docstrings).

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.2. Constrained, well-specified algorithms?
ai-interview.back: - Implementing a known algorithm given a clear spec ("debounce", "trie", "merge sort"). - Glue code between well-known APIs (file → S3, webhook → handler). - Refactors with a clear target ("extract this into a custom hook").

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 4.3. Search and synthesis?
ai-interview.back: - "What are the three ways people typically solve X?" - "What does this error message usually mean in Postgres?" - "Compare TanStack Query vs SWR for my use case."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.1. Novel architecture under real constraints?
ai-interview.back: The LLM doesn't know your team's deploy story, your billing tier, your historical war stories about the queue that took down prod, your manager's risk tolerance, your timeline. It will produce a design that looks reasonable in isolation and is wrong for your context.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.2. Cross-cutting refactors that span a long history?
ai-interview.back: Imagine: "rename a field across 60 files, but only in the new code paths, leaving the legacy paths alone." The LLM doesn't have the institutional memory of which paths are legacy. It will rewrite the wrong subset, confidently.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 5.3. Debugging where the bug is in your understanding?
ai-interview.back: If you're prompting the AI to "fix this race condition" but you've misdiagnosed which two operations are racing, the AI will fix the wrong race condition and the bug will return tomorrow.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.1. The "I love AI" pitfall?
ai-interview.back: You sound like a fan, not an engineer. The interviewer will dig deeper to see if you have any judgment.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.2. The "I'm anti-AI" pitfall?
ai-interview.back: You sound like you're hiding the fact that you don't use it. The interviewer will assume you'll be slower than the AI-fluent candidate.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md: 7.3. The "It depends" pitfall?
ai-interview.back: You sound evasive. "It depends" is only a great answer when you immediately follow with the things it depends on.

ai-interview.back: I just review the AI's code now, like a manager.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 1 — Typing?
ai-interview.back: What it is: keystrokes. Pressing letter keys to put characters in a file.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 2 — Implementation?
ai-interview.back: What it is: turning a clear specification into working code.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: Layer 4 — Judgment?
ai-interview.back: What it is: deciding which design is right for our situation.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.1. Prompt engineering as engineering?
ai-interview.back: Not "prompt hacks." Real engineering: stable system prompts, structured contexts, deterministic output formats, eval suites that score prompts the way unit tests score functions.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.2. Eval-driven development?
ai-interview.back: Writing test cases (sometimes called "evals") that gauge whether an AI-driven feature is doing the right thing. Includes ground-truth datasets, automated scoring, and human-in-the-loop review.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.3. AI code review?
ai-interview.back: The skill of reviewing AI-generated code with a different mental model than human-generated code. AI tends to make different mistakes (overconfident plausible-but-wrong APIs, missing edge cases the spec didn't mention, security mistakes in copy-pasted patterns). Knowing the failure modes is its own discipline.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.4. Cost and latency thinking?
ai-interview.back: LLM calls cost money and add latency. Engineering features that use LLMs at runtime requires understanding token counts, cache strategies, model tiering ("fast model for the easy cases, smart model for the hard ones"), and graceful degradation.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/02-the-skill-stack.md: 4.5. Risk and safety thinking?
ai-interview.back: Knowing where AI must not be in the loop. PII redaction. Prompt-injection defense. Output validation. Regulated-industry constraints.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: '"Walk me through this function." → They pick the most non-ob...'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 'Oh, I'm very careful with AI. I read everything. I run all t...'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 'Yeah, I... used AI for some of this, sorry....'
ai-interview.back: Yeah, I... used AI for some of this, sorry.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.1. The "let me ask the AI" reflex?
ai-interview.back: The crutch user, when asked a question they don't know the answer to, immediately reaches for the AI before thinking. They don't form an initial hypothesis. They don't articulate what they don't know. They just paste-and-pray.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.2. The "the AI said so" defense?
ai-interview.back: The crutch user, when asked why their code does X, says: "That's how the AI suggested it."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.3. The diff-flood acceptance?
ai-interview.back: The crutch user accepts giant diffs without reading them. They watch the AI rewrite 14 files, see "tests pass," and move on. The senior user forces small diffs. They reject "rewrite this whole module" suggestions in favor of "extract this one function" steps they can review.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 2.4. The novelty assumption?
ai-interview.back: The crutch user assumes anything the AI suggests is current best practice. The senior user knows the AI's training data lags reality by months — sometimes years — and that "best practice" is contextual anyway.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.1. Form a hypothesis before you prompt?
ai-interview.back: Before typing a single character to the AI, write down (in your head or in a comment) what you think the answer is. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives you a baseline to compare the AI's answer against.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.2. Always read the diff?
ai-interview.back: Even tiny diffs. Especially tiny diffs — the small ones are where sneaky behavioral changes hide. Make this a reflex, not a chore. Senior engineers can spot a one-character bug in a 200-line diff because they've read enough diffs.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.3. Run the code before trusting it?
ai-interview.back: The AI is confident even when wrong. The compiler isn't. The test runner isn't. Run the code. Run the tests. The number of senior engineers who've shipped a confident-looking AI output without running it is not zero, and they'll all tell you the same horror story when you ask.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 5.4. Keep a log of AI mistakes?
ai-interview.back: For one week, write down every time the AI was wrong. The format: what you asked, what it returned, what was wrong, what the right answer was. By Friday you'll have a personal failure-mode catalog. It will be your best interview prep artifact.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.1. The hypothesis log?
ai-interview.back: For the next 5 working days, write down your hypothesis before prompting the AI. At the end of the week, look at the log. How often were you right? How often was the AI? What patterns of disagreement do you see?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.2. The diff drill?
ai-interview.back: Pick the next 10 AI-generated diffs that land in your code. Force yourself to articulate what each one changes, line by line, before accepting it. It will feel slow. After 10 diffs, it will be habit.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 8.3. The story file?
ai-interview.back: Open interview-stories.md. Write down 5 STAR-format stories from your real work in the last 6 months. At least 2 of them should feature a moment where you did not trust the AI and made the call yourself.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.1. The performative caution?
ai-interview.back: > "Oh, I'm very careful with AI. I read everything. I run all the tests. I never just accept things."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.2. The performative skepticism?
ai-interview.back: > "AI is interesting but I generally write everything myself."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/03-the-mindset-shift.md: 9.3. The performative depth?
ai-interview.back: > "I deeply understand transformer architecture and use that to inform my prompting."

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-01-foundations/README.md: 'Before you can defend AI-assisted development in an intervie...'
ai-interview.back: Before you can defend AI-assisted development in an interview, you have to be able to define it precisely. This chapter gives you that definition — and the vocabulary that goes with it.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'That's a fair question and I think about it a lot. The short...'

ai-interview.back: What's your take on the whole AI-and-developers conversation?" "How do you think your job has changed in the last 18 months?" "Where do you see the most leverage from AI in your day-to-day, and where doesn't it help?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more th...'
ai-interview.back: My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more than it changed whether I'm needed. The typing got automated. The judgment didn't. If anything, the judgment got more important because there are more options to choose between.

ai-interview.back: Where do current AI tools break down for you, technically?" "What's a class of problem you would not let an LLM solve in production?" "How do you reason about when to trust LLM output vs verify it manually?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a...'
ai-interview.back: From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a human engineer over scaling AI tooling for my existing team?" "How do you think about the ROI of a senior engineer vs an AI subscription?" "If we doubled our AI tooling spend, what would still be missing?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help...'
ai-interview.back: AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help if you don't have force. The engineer is the force — the judgment about what to build, the design choices about how it fits, the accountability when it breaks. AI subscription doesn't show up to the postmortem. I do.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'Are you worried about your career?" "Do you think this profe...'
ai-interview.back: Are you worried about your career?" "Do you think this profession will exist in 10 years?" "How do you tell a junior developer to start a career in 2026 with all this happening?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: 'There are seven things humans still bring that AI does not. ...'
ai-interview.back: There are seven things humans still bring that AI does not. Let me run through them quickly.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
ai-interview.back: > "Honestly, I look at this stuff and I wonder why we hire any developers anymore. Convince me." > > "Half my team is being asked to do twice the work because of AI. Are you worth what we pay engineers?" > > "I've seen a single founder ship an entire SaaS app with AI alone. So why do I need a team of you?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
ai-interview.back: - The word "convince", "justify", "prove", "worth". - A short, blunt question without the usual interview pleasantries. - Eye contact that's holding you accountable, not warmly engaging. - They lean back, not forward.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
ai-interview.back: They want to feel that you take the question seriously and have an answer. They don't want a lecture about transformer architecture or a defensive rant. They want a senior engineer's quiet confidence.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
ai-interview.back: > "That's a fair question and I think about it a lot. The short answer is that AI raised the floor of what's possible — but it didn't lower the ceiling of what's required from a senior engineer. Let me explain."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
ai-interview.back: - Don't smirk. - Don't deflect ("that's a complicated question..."). - Don't argue with the premise ("AI hasn't actually replaced developers...") — that's a fight.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
ai-interview.back: > "What's your take on the whole AI-and-developers conversation?" > > "How do you think your job has changed in the last 18 months?" > > "Where do you see the most leverage from AI in your day-to-day, and where doesn't it help?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
ai-interview.back: - "Your take", "how do you see", "what's interesting to you". - Open posture, leaning in. - A genuine pause for you to answer, not a setup for their next point.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
ai-interview.back: This is the most generous framing. The interviewer is curious, conversational, and probably as uncertain as you are. They want a thoughtful exchange, not a defended thesis.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
ai-interview.back: > "My take is that AI has changed what I spend time on, more than it changed whether I'm needed. The typing got automated. The judgment didn't. If anything, the judgment got more important because there are more options to choose between."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
ai-interview.back: - Don't over-prepare and deliver a monologue. They want a chat. - Don't be too humble ("oh, I'm still figuring it out") — you'll sound junior.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
ai-interview.back: > "Where do current AI tools break down for you, technically?" > > "What's a class of problem you would not let an LLM solve in production?" > > "How do you reason about when to trust LLM output vs verify it manually?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
ai-interview.back: - They use precise vocabulary: "in production", "verify", "class of problem". - Specific examples or follow-up questions are likely. - They're often a senior IC, not a manager.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
ai-interview.back: They want concrete technical opinions backed by examples. They are the easiest framing to nail because they're testing your engineering thinking, not your soft skills.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
ai-interview.back: > "There are three classes of work where I won't let the LLM be the primary author: anything that touches money, anything that requires reasoning across more than ~200 lines of unfamiliar context, and anything where the cost of a wrong answer doesn't show up in tests. Let me give you an example for each."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
ai-interview.back: - Don't be vague. Technical interviewers eat vagueness alive. - Don't say "it depends" without immediately specifying what it depends on.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
ai-interview.back: > "From a hiring-manager perspective, why should I prioritize a human engineer over scaling AI tooling for my existing team?" > > "How do you think about the ROI of a senior engineer vs an AI subscription?" > > "If we doubled our AI tooling spend, what would still be missing?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
ai-interview.back: - Words from the budget conversation: "ROI", "priority", "spend", "hire". - Often a director, VP, or non-IC. - They're pattern-matching against other hires they've made.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
ai-interview.back: They want the answer they can paraphrase to their own boss when asked why they hired you instead of buying more AI seats. Give them the line they'll repeat.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
ai-interview.back: > "AI tools are force multipliers. Force multipliers don't help if you don't have force. The engineer is the force — the judgment about what to build, the design choices about how it fits, the accountability when it breaks. AI subscription doesn't show up to the postmortem. I do."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
ai-interview.back: - Don't go technical. They don't care about transformer hallucination rates. - Don't get philosophical. They want concrete value. - Don't be modest. This is the framing where confidence is most rewarded.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: How it sounds?
ai-interview.back: > "Are you worried about your career?" > > "Do you think this profession will exist in 10 years?" > > "How do you tell a junior developer to start a career in 2026 with all this happening?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: Tone signals?
ai-interview.back: - The question is broader than the role. It's about the field, the future, careers. - Often slightly personal — they're vulnerable too, even if they're hiring. - Pause-laden, less briskly transactional than the hostile framing.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What they actually want?
ai-interview.back: They want a sober, honest, optimistic answer. They want to feel the energy in the room shift from anxious to constructive. Done well, this is the framing where you most build genuine rapport.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: The opening sentence?
ai-interview.back: > "I'm thoughtful about it but not worried. The work is changing — not vanishing. The people who will struggle are the ones who tied their identity to typing code. The people who'll thrive are the ones who tied their identity to making good engineering decisions. I'm in the second camp, and I'd advise a junior to be in the second camp too."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md: What not to do?
ai-interview.back: - Don't catastrophize. - Don't be saccharine ("I think humans will always have a place...") — sounds rehearsed. - Don't pivot too quickly to mechanics. This framing wants 30 seconds of genuine reflection first.

ai-interview.back: AI is genuinely good at producing text that looks like code. Producing text isn't the same as engineering. There are seven things engineering requires that AI does not do — and probably won't, in the time horizons that matter for this hiring decision. Let me run through them.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec sho...'
ai-interview.back: AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec should be is engineering.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI knows code. Engineers know this codebase, this team, and ...'
ai-interview.back: AI knows code. Engineers know this codebase, this team, and this customer.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new m...'
ai-interview.back: AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new module. In our codebase Redux had been deprecated for 18 months in favor of Zustand. The AI couldn't know that. A new hire who blindly accepted that suggestion would have wasted a sprint adding a deprecated dependency.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because ...'
ai-interview.back: AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because it doesn't own consequences.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at t...'
ai-interview.back: AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at the next 2 years.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings ...'
ai-interview.back: AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings to keep the API simple. That works at 8 users. At 80 users we'd have been parsing strings on every read. I made the call to use a normalized schema. AI optimized for the prompt; I optimized for two years from now.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in thos...'
ai-interview.back: Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in those conversations.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real const...'
ai-interview.back: PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real constraint was a customer demo on Tuesday. The right move was a smaller scope shipped Monday rather than the full feature shipped Friday. AI doesn't catch that — there's no prompt that surfaces a demo two states away.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good f...'
ai-interview.back: AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good for our situation.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI generated a function that walked the data twice — once to...'
ai-interview.back: AI generated a function that walked the data twice — once to filter, once to map. Functionally fine. For 8 teams. We had a planned migration to 800 in Q2. I rewrote it as a single pass, not because the original was 'wrong', but because I could see the next refactor.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature i...'
ai-interview.back: At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature is the value.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'AI raised the floor of what's possible. It hasn't lowered th...'
ai-interview.back: AI raised the floor of what's possible. It hasn't lowered the ceiling of what's required.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a ...'
ai-interview.back: It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a senior was someone who could ship features fast. Today they have to do that and judge AI output, and design for AI-assisted teams, and evaluate when AI shouldn't be in the loop. The job got harder, not easier.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: 'Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the ga...'
ai-interview.back: Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the game. But I'm not asking you to take my word — I'm describing observable, daily failures of AI output that I and every other engineer I know spent today fixing. The case rests on the data, not on my motive.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI optimizes the spec it's given. Deciding what the spec should be is engineering."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "On my last project, the PM asked for a 'scoring system.' The naive AI prompt would have produced a perfectly working scorer. But the actual job was to choose between three definitions of 'score' — points-per-pick, accuracy-percentage, or playoff-style — each of which served a different storyline for the user. I went and watched the league members react to mockups before any code got written. AI didn't sit in those meetings."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI knows code. Engineers know this codebase, this team, and this customer."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
ai-interview.back: AI has the public internet's worth of code in its head. What it does not have: your codebase's local conventions, your team's deploy story, the war story from last quarter when the queue overflowed, the senior engineer who refuses to touch the legacy module, the customer who pays you 40% of revenue and hates change. Those facts shape every real engineering decision. AI's suggestions are correct in the average codebase. Your team is not the average codebase. Adapting AI suggestions to local context — knowing what to keep, what to throw out, what to reshape — that's the work.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "AI suggested I use Redux for the state management on a new module. In our codebase Redux had been deprecated for 18 months in favor of Zustand. The AI couldn't know that. A new hire who blindly accepted that suggestion would have wasted a sprint adding a deprecated dependency."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI can list trade-offs. AI doesn't make trade-offs, because it doesn't own consequences."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
ai-interview.back: When you make a trade-off — pick A over B — you accept that A's downsides will surface in production, in code review, in the on-call channel at 2am. The AI does not show up to those moments. It doesn't get paged. It doesn't get reviewed. It doesn't have a reputation that suffers when the trade-off ages badly. Engineering decisions live with their consequences for years. The accountability for those consequences is the most valuable thing humans bring. When the postmortem asks "why did we choose this?", the AI cannot answer. You can.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "We had to choose between caching at the CDN, the application server, or the client. AI laid out the three trade-offs cleanly. The decision required knowing that our CDN bill was already over budget, that our app servers had spare CPU but tight memory, and that 30% of our users had unstable connections. None of that was in the AI's prompt. We picked the application-server caching. When it later created a memory pressure issue, I owned the decision — and the fix."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI is great at the next 200 lines. Engineering is great at the next 2 years."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
ai-interview.back: LLMs operate on a context window. Your project operates on a multi-year arc. Decisions you make now — schema design, API contract, module boundaries — get harder to reverse with every passing month. The skill of saying "this looks fine for the demo but it'll be technical debt for the next five years, let's spend an extra hour and do it right" is temporal judgment. AI doesn't have it. It doesn't even know the current date with confidence. It cannot weigh present convenience against future maintenance. Senior engineers do that every hour.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "AI suggested we store user picks as comma-separated strings to keep the API simple. That works at 8 users. At 80 users we'd have been parsing strings on every read. I made the call to use a normalized schema. AI optimized for the prompt; I optimized for two years from now."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Software is mostly built in conversations. AI is not in those conversations."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "PM asked for a feature 'by Friday.' I noticed the real constraint was a customer demo on Tuesday. The right move was a smaller scope shipped Monday rather than the full feature shipped Friday. AI doesn't catch that — there's no prompt that surfaces a demo two states away."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI can write code. Engineers know whether the code is good for our situation."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
ai-interview.back: AI output is plausibly-correct on the surface and frequently subtly wrong. Spotting the wrong subset requires taste — knowing which patterns wear well, which abstractions leak, which "clean" architectures will fight you in 3 months. Taste isn't innate; it's earned by reading a lot of code, shipping a lot of code, and watching it age. AI does not have taste. It has averages. The skill of "this is correct but it's the wrong correct for us" is irreducibly human.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "AI generated a function that walked the data twice — once to filter, once to map. Functionally fine. For 8 teams. We had a planned migration to 800 in Q2. I rewrote it as a single pass, not because the original was 'wrong', but because I could see the next refactor."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "At the end of every PR is a human signature. The signature is the value."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Elaboration (30 seconds)?
ai-interview.back: When code ships, someone owns it. The person on the call. The person who explains it to the auditor. The person whose name is on the postmortem. AI cannot be that person. Not legally, not socially, not professionally. As long as software has consequences — and it always will, especially in regulated, security-critical, or revenue-bearing contexts — there will be a human at the bottom of the trust chain. Engineers are the trust layer. AI tools are below us in the stack, like compilers and linters. Useful, important, not interchangeable with the engineer.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Concrete example?
ai-interview.back: > "I shipped a payments change last quarter. The bug it introduced cost us four hours of partial-charge confusion. The remediation involved a written explanation to compliance, a refund script, and a personal apology to a customer. There is no version of that incident response where 'the AI did it' is an acceptable sentence. I owned it because I had to."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "But AI is getting better fast."?
ai-interview.back: > "It is. So is the bar for senior engineers. Five years ago a senior was someone who could ship features fast. Today they have to do that and judge AI output, and design for AI-assisted teams, and evaluate when AI shouldn't be in the loop. The job got harder, not easier."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "Couldn't I just use a junior plus AI for the price of you?"?
ai-interview.back: > "You can. You'll get a lot of typing-rate productivity. The first time the AI's suggestion conflicts with a deploy story, a customer constraint, or a security review, the junior won't have the judgment to push back. That's the sound of technical debt accumulating quietly. I'd argue you save by hiring me; the bill comes due either way."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "What if I told you AI will fully replace developers in 10 years?"?
ai-interview.back: > "I can't disprove a 10-year prediction. I can tell you that none of the AI-replacement timelines I've seen come from people who are currently shipping production code with AI tools daily. The people closest to the work are the most measured about its limits. I trust that signal more than I trust the timelines."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/02-the-seven-pillars.md: Objection: "Aren't you just defending your job?"?
ai-interview.back: > "Sure, partly. I'd be lying if I said I had no skin in the game. But I'm not asking you to take my word — I'm describing observable, daily failures of AI output that I and every other engineer I know spent today fixing. The case rests on the data, not on my motive."

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you: Do you have a 30-second version of Case 5 (compliance) ready? It is the highest-leverage story
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.
