ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'Soundbites win the abstract argument. Stories win the room. ...'
ai-interview.back: Soundbites win the abstract argument. Stories win the room. This lesson gives you eight short, memorable cases — each tied to a pillar from Lesson 2 — that you can adapt as your own.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist....'
ai-interview.back: And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist...

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 'And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spot...'
ai-interview.back: And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spotted the issue...

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: You asked the AI to fix a failing test. The AI's diff "fixed" the test by changing the test's assertion to match the buggy code, instead of fixing the bug. The tests passed. The bug shipped.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "Working late, I asked the AI to fix a failing test. It returned a diff that made the test pass — by changing the expected value to match the broken code's actual output. Tests went green. The bug stayed. If I had accepted that diff without reading it, the bug would have shipped. The AI optimized for what I asked — green tests — not for what I meant — correct behavior. After that I made it a rule: when the AI fixes a test, the diff must touch the implementation, not the assertion."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: It is a tiny, plausible story. The interviewer instantly believes it because they have seen something like it. The lesson is concrete: distinguish passing tests from correct code. It maps cleanly to Pillar 6 (quality judgment).

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Find a real instance. Search your git log for phrases like "fix test" or "test now passes". Half of every developer's history has at least one of these moments. Use yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: The AI suggested a popular library to solve a problem. The library had been deprecated months ago, replaced by a newer one. You caught it because you knew your codebase had already moved.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "AI suggested I use the older state-management library that everyone Googled in 2022. Reasonable in the abstract. Our codebase had migrated off it eighteen months ago — there was an internal RFC and everything. The AI did not know the RFC existed. A new hire would have followed the suggestion and added a deprecated dependency in their first week. I caught it because I had been on the team during the migration. That is the kind of context that does not fit in any prompt I could write."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: It demonstrates Pillar 2 (context) without sounding self-important. The implicit message: I am valuable because I have been here long enough to have caught this.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Look for any place in your team's history where a library was swapped, a pattern was deprecated, a custom utility replaced an industry standard, or "we do not do it that way anymore" is true. Every team has these. Mine yours for the story.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: The AI suggested a quick, denormalized data shape that would have been fine at small scale and painful at larger scale. You picked the slower-now / better-later option.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "The AI suggested storing user picks as comma-separated strings — one column, simple to read, simple to write. It worked for the eight teams in our beta. We had a planned scale to roughly 800 teams in Q2. At that scale we would have been parsing strings on every read, and adding a 'remove pick X' feature would have been a regex nightmare. I went with a normalized join table. Slower to type, faster to live with. The AI optimized for the demo. I optimized for the next two years."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: Pillar 4 (long-horizon judgment) is one of the harder ones to make concrete. This story does it. The phrase "AI optimized for the demo, I optimized for the next two years" is a quotable line interviewers will remember.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Look for any decision where you traded short-term ease for long-term maintainability. Pagination instead of "load all", a normalized DB instead of denormalized JSON, a state library instead of a useState blob. Any of these work.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: A PM asked for a feature by Friday. You discovered the real constraint was a Tuesday customer demo. You shipped a smaller scope on Monday — useful for the demo — and finished the rest at a sane pace.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "PM said: 'I need this feature by Friday.' I asked why Friday — turns out there was a customer demo on Tuesday and Friday was just a buffer. The actual ask was: have something demo-able on Monday. We scoped a much smaller version that hit the demo's narrative, shipped Monday, and built the rest at a normal pace through the next sprint. The AI cannot ask 'why Friday?' because it is not in the conversation. The five-minute clarifying question saved a weekend and made the demo tighter."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: This is the most repeatable Pillar 5 story (cross-team translation). It also makes you sound like a senior who manages stakeholders, not just a coder.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Look for any case where you reframed a request after a clarifying conversation. The reframing did not have to be dramatic — even "let me make sure I understand the goal" counts. The story lands as long as the clarifying question changed the plan.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: You shipped a change. It introduced a bug with real customer impact. You owned the response — wrote the postmortem, the customer email, the audit-trail update. The story is not the bug; it is the ownership.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "I shipped a payments change last quarter. It mishandled a partial-charge edge case and caused four hours of confused customers. The remediation involved a written explanation to compliance, a refund script, and personal apology emails to the affected accounts. There is no version of that incident response where 'the AI did it' is an acceptable sentence. I owned the change because I was the one who hit merge. That ownership — not the typing — is what I am hired for."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: This is the strongest Pillar 7 (accountability) story. It works in any framing because every interviewer respects engineers who own their incidents. Critically, it lets you talk about a bug without sounding like you ship bugs — because the focus is on the response, not the failure.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Find an incident from your real history. Tell the version where you owned it well. If you have only ever shipped flawless code, find a friend's incident and tell theirs (with credit) — but ideally find your own. Postmortems are gold for interviews.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: A caching decision had three valid options. The AI listed them. The decision required knowledge AI did not have. You made the call, owned it, and adjusted when reality bit back.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "We had to choose where to cache: CDN edge, application server, or browser. AI laid out the three trade-offs cleanly. The decision needed three pieces of context the AI did not have: our CDN bill was already over budget, our app servers had spare CPU but tight memory, and 30 percent of our users had unstable connections. We chose application-server caching. It later created a memory pressure issue, and the fix was a TTL change. I owned the decision and the fix. The AI listed; I chose."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: Pillar 3 (trade-off ownership) is abstract until you tell a story like this. The list of three context items — budget, memory, network reliability — is the punchline. None of those facts come from a prompt. They come from being on the team.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Find a decision in your work that had three or more valid technical answers. Trace the decision to facts that lived outside the code: budget, headcount, regulatory constraints, customer mix, deploy story. Those facts are the story.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: The PM asked for "a scoring system." Three different scoring designs were equally valid. You went and watched users react to mockups before any code was written. The right design surfaced from the research.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 30-second delivery?
ai-interview.back: > "PM asked for 'a scoring system.' AI could have produced any of three working scorers — points-per-pick, accuracy-percentage, or playoff-style — and each was correct in isolation. They were not interchangeable for the user, though. I built three quick mockups, watched five league members react to each, and discovered the playoff-style version generated the most table conversation. We shipped that one. None of the user reactions were inferable from the prompt. They came from being in a room with humans."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: Pillar 1 (problem framing) is the highest-altitude pillar and the most senior-coded. This story shows you operating there: you went upstream of the spec, did discovery, and let it shape the implementation.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Look for any feature where you did not start with "implement X" but with "what should X be?" Even informal user research counts: a Slack poll, a hallway conversation, a five-minute prototype shown to a teammate. Any of those replace pure prompting.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: What happened (template)?
ai-interview.back: You needed to change behavior in a module that had been load-bearing for years. AI tried, but the change rippled through code paths it could not see. You had to step in, scope the change manually, and ship in pieces.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: Why it lands?
ai-interview.back: This story carries Pillars 2 and 4 (context + long-horizon). It also tells the interviewer you can work safely in legacy code, which is one of the most valuable senior signals.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: How to make it your own?
ai-interview.back: Find your equivalent legacy module. Every codebase has one. The story does not need to be dramatic — even "I scoped down what the AI suggested because two of those files have weird history" is enough.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.1. Making the AI sound dumb?
ai-interview.back: > "And of course the AI just made up an API that doesn't exist..."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.2. Making yourself sound heroic?
ai-interview.back: > "And I, brilliant senior engineer that I am, immediately spotted the issue..."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.3. Telling stories you cannot defend?
ai-interview.back: If your story includes "and we used Postgres-specific extension X", be ready for: "Which one? Why?" Do not tell stories whose details you cannot improvise around. Stick to your real history.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-02-the-big-question/03-real-world-cases.md: 12.4. Making them too long?
ai-interview.back: A 30-second story stays a story. A 90-second story becomes a monologue. Cut ruthlessly. The interviewer can ask follow-ups for detail. Your job is the headline.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: The verb library?
ai-interview.back: | If you want... | Use this verb | | -------------- | ------------- | | Code that does not exist yet | Create / Generate / Add | | Code that exists but should change | Refactor / Replace / Rewrite | | Code split into smaller code | Extract | | Understanding | Explain / Walk through | | Choices laid out | Compare / List approaches to | | A bug found | Diagnose / Trace | | A bug fixed | Patch (with the bug specified) | | Tests added | Cover / Test | | Names changed | Rename |

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Good?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a <LoadingState> component to components/ui/loading-state.tsx. Default export. Props: label (optional, defaults to 'Loading...'), size ('sm' | 'md' | 'lg', defaults to 'md'). Render a centered spinner with the label below it."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: What to specify?
ai-interview.back: - Where it lives. File path or directory. - What it is. Component / function / hook / type / config / test. - Its name. Pin the name to avoid the model inventing a verbose one. - Its API surface. Props, parameters, return type. - Its rendering or behavior. One sentence is enough.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Examples that work?
ai-interview.back: - "Do not introduce new dependencies." - "Do not change the existing styling." - "Do not modify any file outside components/ui/." - "Do not introduce a Context — pass props." - "Do not generate tests in this turn — I will ask separately." - "Do not refactor unrelated code." - "Do not change the public API of useSnookerScores." - "Do not add comments unless I ask."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Why this matters in interviews?
ai-interview.back: Interviewers reading your prompts can tell who has been burned by AI sprawl. Constraint clauses are the scar tissue of experienced AI users. Show your scars.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: What NOT to include?
ai-interview.back: - Your whole codebase. The model gets confused by noise. - Irrelevant history. "Previously we tried X..." → only mention if the failed attempt is informative. - Personal context. "My PM is annoying about this..." → the model does not need this.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Examples?
ai-interview.back: - "The empty array case must render an explicit empty state — not just nothing." - "Negative numbers should not be possible — clamp to zero in the helper." - "This API call may return 404 if the league has not started — handle that explicitly." - "The user might have unfinished picks. Treat unfinished as a third state, not as wrong." - "Avoid Date.now() — use the clock parameter that is passed in for testability."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Common formats and when to use each?
ai-interview.back: | Format | When | | ------ | ---- | | Unified diff | Refactor / patch / change to existing file | | Single complete file | Brand-new file | | List of file paths + contents | Multi-file scaffold | | Explanation prose, no code | "Compare three approaches" | | Step-by-step plan | Before any code is written, for big features | | Shell commands | Setup, install, scaffold tasks | | JSON block | Structured data extraction |

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: Why this matters?
ai-interview.back: Without a format, the model picks one. Half the time it is wrong for your workflow. Specifying the format makes the output immediately usable — paste-friendly, diff-applicable, or grep-able.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v1 — Add the deliverable?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v2 — Add constraints?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v3 — Add failure modes?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder. The button must remain keyboard-focusable while loading (focus ring visible) but click handlers should be no-ops. Announce the state change with aria-busy."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: v4 — Add output format?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a loading state to <PrimaryButton> in components/ui/PrimaryButton.tsx. When isLoading is true, show a spinner inside the button and disable it. Do not change the existing prop API. Do not add a new dependency for the spinner — use <Spinner> from the same folder. The button must remain keyboard-focusable while loading (focus ring visible) but click handlers should be no-ops. Announce the state change with aria-busy. Return a unified diff. No prose."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.1. Over-specifying?
ai-interview.back: A prompt with twelve constraints, three pages of context, and seven failure modes is overkill. The model gets confused. Stop at six components, with two or three items per component.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.3. Mixing modes?
ai-interview.back: > "Refactor this and also add tests and explain how it works and update the docs."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md: 13.4. Prompting before thinking?
ai-interview.back: The fastest way to write a slow prompt is to skip the thinking step. Spend 30 seconds composing a hypothesis ("I think this should be X") before writing the prompt. The hypothesis improves the prompt.

ai-interview.back: Twelve prompts cover ninety percent of real engineering work with AI. This lesson is the library. Memorize the shapes. Adapt the words.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN O...'
ai-interview.back: I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [3 TO 5 USER ACTIONS]. Do not write code yet. Confirm you understand the data and the actions, propose a tab UI, and call out anything ambiguous.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt....'
ai-interview.back: My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt. I tell the model what I think the problem is and ask it to push back. Forces ambiguities to surface in 30 seconds instead of after I have shipped the wrong thing.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — n...'
ai-interview.back: Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — no any, no implicit optional fields. For each interface, comment any non-obvious invariant. Use composition rather than inheritance when interfaces share fields.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They m...'
ai-interview.back: Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They make every subsequent prompt easier because the model has shapes to refer to.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTP...'
ai-interview.back: Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects. No I/O. No framework imports. Add JSDoc comments explaining each. Output as a single .ts file.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the ...'
ai-interview.back: I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the codebase that survives every refactor, and it is trivially testable. I will not start UI work until the logic is solid.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a ...'
ai-interview.back: Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a functional React component with explicit props typed from [TYPE FILE]. No useState. No useMemo. Inline styles. Do not extract sub-components yet.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Render a basic StandingsTab from an array of TeamWithScores....'
ai-interview.back: Render a basic StandingsTab from an array of TeamWithScores. Use a plain HTML table. No useState, no useMemo, no extracted sub-components. Show team name and total. Inline styles only — match the green/yellow palette in lib/constants.ts.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data ...'
ai-interview.back: I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data is real. Inline styles. No abstractions. Then I let abstractions earn their way in.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT at FILEPATH]. Do no...'
ai-interview.back: Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT at FILEPATH]. Do not redesign anything. Do not refactor unrelated code. If the change touches more than three files, stop and tell me which files first. Return a unified diff.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Add a per-round column to the standings table in components/...'
ai-interview.back: Add a per-round column to the standings table in components/tabs/StandingsTab.tsx. Show R1 / R2 / QF / SF / Final scores. Do not redesign the table. Do not extract sub-components. If this touches more than three files, stop and tell me. Diff format.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not re...'
ai-interview.back: This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not redesign' clause and the 'stop if more than three files' clause are scar tissue from the times the AI helpfully rewrote my whole module.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only ...'
ai-interview.back: Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only [WHAT MOVES]. Do not change behavior. Do not change the public API of [PUBLIC SURFACE]. Keep all styling identical. Add no new dependencies.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Refactor components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx into an orchest...'
ai-interview.back: Refactor components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns activeTab and selectedTeam with useState. Tab components receive props. Do not change behavior. Do not change styling. Tests must continue to pass.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constra...'
ai-interview.back: Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constraints. Behavior must not change. Styling must not change. Public API must not change. Without those clauses, the AI helpfully 'improves' things and the diff becomes unreviewable.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPEC...'
ai-interview.back: Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPECTED BEHAVIOR]. Reproducer: [STEPS]. Hypothesis: [YOUR GUESS]. Confirm or refute the hypothesis. Then patch the smallest part of the code that fixes it. Do not change tests.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. E...'
ai-interview.back: I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives the model something to confirm or refute. Without it, the model guesses, and guessing is what got me into the bug.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cov...'
ai-interview.back: Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cover these specific cases: [LIST]. Use [TEST FRAMEWORK]. Mirror the style of [EXISTING TEST FILE if any]. One assertion per test.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.t...'
ai-interview.back: Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.ts. Cases: correct pick returns 3; wrong pick returns 1; unplayed match returns null. Mirror the style of lib/utils.test.ts. One assertion per test. Use describe/it. Do not test calculateTeamScores in this turn.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model wi...'
ai-interview.back: Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model will happily generate twenty tests of weak quality. I prefer three tests that hit the boundary cases I named.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraint...'
ai-interview.back: I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: [LIST]. List three approaches with their trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface any constraints I missed.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick....'
ai-interview.back: When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick. The 'list three approaches' framing forces it to surface options I would have missed. The choice still belongs to me.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [ST...'
ai-interview.back: Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [STYLE]. Length: [LIMIT]. Cover [SECTIONS]. Do not invent features. If anything is unclear from the code, list questions instead of guessing.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will hap...'
ai-interview.back: The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will happily document behavior the code does not have. The 'list questions instead' clause turns documentation into a sanity check on the code itself.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it ...'
ai-interview.back: Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it produced: [OUTPUT]. Here is what I actually wanted: [DESIRED]. Suggest a stronger prompt that closes the gap. Explain which of the six prompt components it strengthens.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new co...'
ai-interview.back: Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new component called Loader at the wrong path with new dependencies. Wanted: a small change to PrimaryButton that adds isLoading prop. Suggest a stronger prompt and explain which components it strengthens.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, no...'
ai-interview.back: When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, not the model. I have a meta-prompt that asks the AI to critique my prompt and suggest a better one. It works almost every time.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 'Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. T...'
ai-interview.back: Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. The structure is well-known, the tone is hard to nail, and the model is great at both. I always edit the draft, but the draft saves me an hour.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: You are about to start a new project. Before any code, you want to confirm the model understands the problem.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "I want to build a [DOMAIN] app. The data is [DATA SHAPE IN ONE PARAGRAPH]. The user can [3 TO 5 USER ACTIONS]. Do not write code yet. Confirm you understand the data and the actions, propose a tab UI, and call out anything ambiguous."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "I want to build a fantasy snooker league app. The data is eight teams, each picking match winners across five rounds; matches have winners or are unplayed; scoring is 3 for a correct pick, 1 for wrong, null for unplayed. The user can see standings, drill into one team's picks, see analytics charts, and switch rounds. Do not write code yet. Confirm understanding, propose a five-tab UI, and ask any clarifying questions."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "My first prompt on a new project is always a no-code prompt. I tell the model what I think the problem is and ask it to push back. Forces ambiguities to surface in 30 seconds instead of after I have shipped the wrong thing."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: You know the entities. You want strict types before any UI exists.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Define TypeScript interfaces for [ENTITIES]. Strict mode — no any, no implicit optional fields. For each interface, comment any non-obvious invariant. Use composition rather than inheritance when interfaces share fields."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Define TypeScript interfaces for Match, Team, PlayerInfo, ScoreDetail, TeamScores. Strict — no any. Match.winner is optional only when the match has not been played. Team.r1, r2, qf, sf are arrays where index i corresponds to round-N match index i — comment that invariant explicitly."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Strict types up front are the cheapest tax I can pay. They make every subsequent prompt easier because the model has shapes to refer to."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: You want testable, side-effect-free functions before any React.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Write [N] pure functions that take [INPUTS] and return [OUTPUTS]. No side effects. No I/O. No framework imports. Add JSDoc comments explaining each. Output as a single .ts file."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Write two pure functions: scorePick(pick: string, match: Match): number | null and calculateTeamScores(team: Team): TeamScores. scorePick returns 3 if pick equals match.winner, 1 if pick is wrong, null if match has no winner. calculateTeamScores aggregates across all five rounds. No I/O. No React. Output as lib/scoring.ts."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "I write the pure logic before any UI. It is the part of the codebase that survives every refactor, and it is trivially testable. I will not start UI work until the logic is solid."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: You have data and types. You want the smallest possible thing on screen to prove the data is real.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Render the simplest possible visualization of [DATA]. Use a functional React component with explicit props typed from [TYPE FILE]. No useState. No useMemo. Inline styles. Do not extract sub-components yet."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Render a basic StandingsTab from an array of TeamWithScores. Use a plain HTML table. No useState, no useMemo, no extracted sub-components. Show team name and total. Inline styles only — match the green/yellow palette in lib/constants.ts."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "I always start with the smallest thing that proves the data is real. Inline styles. No abstractions. Then I let abstractions earn their way in."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: You have a working component. You want to add one feature without redesigning anything.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Add [ONE FEATURE] to [EXISTING COMPONENT at FILEPATH]. Do not redesign anything. Do not refactor unrelated code. If the change touches more than three files, stop and tell me which files first. Return a unified diff."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a per-round column to the standings table in components/tabs/StandingsTab.tsx. Show R1 / R2 / QF / SF / Final scores. Do not redesign the table. Do not extract sub-components. If this touches more than three files, stop and tell me. Diff format."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "This is the most useful prompt in my library. The 'do not redesign' clause and the 'stop if more than three files' clause are scar tissue from the times the AI helpfully rewrote my whole module."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Refactor [CURRENT FILE] into an [TARGET PATTERN]. Move only [WHAT MOVES]. Do not change behavior. Do not change the public API of [PUBLIC SURFACE]. Keep all styling identical. Add no new dependencies."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Refactor components/SnookerFantasyLeague.tsx into an orchestrator pattern. The orchestrator owns activeTab and selectedTeam with useState. Tab components receive props. Do not change behavior. Do not change styling. Tests must continue to pass."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Refactors are the prompts where I am strictest about constraints. Behavior must not change. Styling must not change. Public API must not change. Without those clauses, the AI helpfully 'improves' things and the diff becomes unreviewable."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: A test is failing or a behavior is wrong. You want a hypothesis-driven fix.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Bug: [SPECIFIC OBSERVED BEHAVIOR]. Expected: [SPECIFIC EXPECTED BEHAVIOR]. Reproducer: [STEPS]. Hypothesis: [YOUR GUESS]. Confirm or refute the hypothesis. Then patch the smallest part of the code that fixes it. Do not change tests."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Bug: scorePick returns 1 for unplayed matches. Expected: it should return null. Reproducer: scorePick('Williams', { p1: 'Williams', p2: 'Wakelin' }). Hypothesis: the function checks 'pick === winner' before checking 'has winner', and falls through to the 1-return. Confirm or refute. Patch only lib/scoring.ts. Do not modify tests."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "I always include my own hypothesis when debugging with AI. Even a wrong hypothesis is useful — it gives the model something to confirm or refute. Without it, the model guesses, and guessing is what got me into the bug."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Write [N] [unit / integration / E2E] tests for [TARGET]. Cover these specific cases: [LIST]. Use [TEST FRAMEWORK]. Mirror the style of [EXISTING TEST FILE if any]. One assertion per test."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Write three Vitest unit tests for scorePick in lib/scoring.ts. Cases: correct pick returns 3; wrong pick returns 1; unplayed match returns null. Mirror the style of lib/utils.test.ts. One assertion per test. Use describe/it. Do not test calculateTeamScores in this turn."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Tests are where I am most explicit about cases. The model will happily generate twenty tests of weak quality. I prefer three tests that hit the boundary cases I named."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: A decision needs to be made. You want options and trade-offs without a default pick.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "I need to decide [DECISION]. Constraints: [LIST]. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: [LIST]. List three approaches with their trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface any constraints I missed."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "I need to decide where to cache: CDN, application server, or browser. Constraints: total monthly cost cap is $X, app servers have spare CPU and tight memory, 30% of users are on flaky networks. Constraints I am not willing to negotiate: no third-party CDN beyond our existing Cloudfront. List three approaches with trade-offs. Do not pick one. Surface anything I missed."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "When I am genuinely undecided, I tell the model not to pick. The 'list three approaches' framing forces it to surface options I would have missed. The choice still belongs to me."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Generate [DOC TYPE] for [TARGET]. Audience: [WHO]. Tone: [STYLE]. Length: [LIMIT]. Cover [SECTIONS]. Do not invent features. If anything is unclear from the code, list questions instead of guessing."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Generate a README for the lib/scoring.ts module. Audience: a junior engineer joining next month. Tone: technical but friendly. Length: under 300 words. Cover the two functions, the invariant about array index correspondence, and one example call each. Do not invent features. List anything unclear instead of guessing."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "The 'do not invent features' clause is critical. AI will happily document behavior the code does not have. The 'list questions instead' clause turns documentation into a sanity check on the code itself."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: When you reach for it?
ai-interview.back: A prompt of yours produced bad output. You want a better prompt.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Here is the prompt I wrote: [PROMPT]. Here is the output it produced: [OUTPUT]. Here is what I actually wanted: [DESIRED]. Suggest a stronger prompt that closes the gap. Explain which of the six prompt components it strengthens."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "Prompt: 'add a loading state to my button.' Output: a new component called Loader at the wrong path with new dependencies. Wanted: a small change to PrimaryButton that adds isLoading prop. Suggest a stronger prompt and explain which components it strengthens."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "When a prompt produces bad output, the prompt is the bug, not the model. I have a meta-prompt that asks the AI to critique my prompt and suggest a better one. It works almost every time."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "We shipped a bug: [DESCRIBE]. Customer impact: [DESCRIBE]. Root cause: [WHAT YOU FOUND]. Detection: [HOW WE FOUND IT]. Remediation: [WHAT WE DID]. Generate a postmortem document with Summary, Timeline, Root Cause, Contributing Factors, What Went Well, What Could Improve, Action Items. Be honest about what we missed. No blame language."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Real example?
ai-interview.back: > "We shipped a payments bug: partial-charge edge case mishandled. Impact: 4 hours, ~50 affected accounts. Root cause: missing null check after gateway response. Detection: customer support ticket, then alerting fired. Remediation: hotfix + refund script. Generate a full postmortem doc. Honest about what we missed. No blame."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: Interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Postmortems are one of the highest-value places to use AI. The structure is well-known, the tone is hard to nail, and the model is great at both. I always edit the draft, but the draft saves me an hour."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.1. Wrong pattern for the job?
ai-interview.back: Using the Refactor template when you really need a Design template — you got code when you wanted options.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.2. Stuffing two patterns into one prompt?
ai-interview.back: "Refactor X and add tests and update the docs" — three patterns crammed into one. Output is mediocre across all three. Run them as three prompts.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/02-the-twelve-patterns.md: 16.3. Skipping the pattern step?
ai-interview.back: Prompting from a blank cursor instead of starting from a template. Slower, weaker output. Even five seconds of "which pattern is this?" improves the prompt.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Engineers who can reject AI output cleanly are worth more th...'
ai-interview.back: Engineers who can reject AI output cleanly are worth more than engineers who only know how to accept it. This lesson teaches you both the discipline and how to talk about it.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Show me you can tell the difference between AI output you sh...'
ai-interview.back: Show me you can tell the difference between AI output you should accept and AI output you should reject.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'I asked for a debounced search. The diff implemented a throt...'
ai-interview.back: I asked for a debounced search. The diff implemented a throttled search. They look similar; they are not the same. Reject.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff used getServerSideProps in a Next.js 14 App Router ...'
ai-interview.back: The diff used getServerSideProps in a Next.js 14 App Router project. The right behavior, the wrong era. Reject.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'I asked to add a prop. The diff touched eleven files includi...'
ai-interview.back: I asked to add a prop. The diff touched eleven files including a 'while we are here' refactor of the parent. Reject and re-prompt with constraints.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff wrapped my hook in a context provider 'for future f...'
ai-interview.back: The diff wrapped my hook in a context provider 'for future flexibility.' We do not have a use case for that flexibility. Reject — over-engineering.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'The diff used a one-line ternary with array spread that I ha...'
ai-interview.back: The diff used a one-line ternary with array spread that I had to stare at for two minutes. Reject — even if the spread is correct, code I cannot read in three seconds is code I will misread under pressure.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling......'
ai-interview.back: Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling... but also use the existing Spinner... but also...

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'Show me a recent diff from your AI tool that you rejected. W...'
ai-interview.back: Show me a recent diff from your AI tool that you rejected. Walk me through why.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'How do you handle AI hallucinations?...'
ai-interview.back: How do you handle AI hallucinations?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 'AI sometimes gives wrong code. Doesn't that worry you?...'
ai-interview.back: AI sometimes gives wrong code. Doesn't that worry you?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.1. The diff does the wrong thing?
ai-interview.back: The most basic reason. The output works, but it is not what you asked for. The model interpreted your prompt differently than you intended.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.2. The diff does the right thing wrongly?
ai-interview.back: The output is functionally correct but uses an approach inappropriate for your codebase: deprecated APIs, the wrong abstraction, fighting your existing patterns.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.3. The diff is too big?
ai-interview.back: The output sprawls into files you did not ask about. Even if every line is correct, the change is unreviewable. Force a smaller diff.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.4. The diff hides complexity?
ai-interview.back: The output works but introduces an abstraction (a wrapper, a context, a generic) you did not ask for and that adds more cognitive cost than it saves.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 2.5. You cannot explain a part of it?
ai-interview.back: The output works and looks reasonable, but there is at least one block you cannot walk through line by line. If you cannot explain it, you cannot ship it.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.1. You keep adding "but also..." in follow-ups?
ai-interview.back: > "Add a loading state... but also do not change the styling... but also use the existing Spinner... but also..."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.2. The output keeps drifting in the same wrong direction?
ai-interview.back: If three iterations all produce variants of the wrong thing, your prompt is anchoring the model toward that wrong thing. Often a single misleading word is the culprit. "Make this more efficient" often produces premature optimization. "Make this cleaner" often produces unnecessary abstractions. Audit your verbs.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.3. You find yourself debugging the prompt instead of the code?
ai-interview.back: If you have spent more than two minutes deciding whether to reject a diff, the prompt was probably under-specified. Reject the diff, rewrite the prompt, run again. Faster than wrestling with the output.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 3.4. The output is plausible but addresses a different problem?
ai-interview.back: The most insidious failure. The diff compiles, runs, and looks good — but it solves a problem adjacent to yours. This means your prompt was ambiguous about the problem itself. Go back and write the problem statement more precisely.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 4.2. Reject in writing, briefly?
ai-interview.back: When you reject a diff, write one line about why. To yourself. To the PR description if relevant. "Rejected because it changed the public API of useScores; constraint added to next prompt." This builds your personal failure-mode catalog and signals the discipline to anyone reading your history.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 4.3. Reject without rage?
ai-interview.back: Frustration is contagious. If you find yourself reflexively dismissing AI output, your prompts will get worse and your suspicion will get less calibrated. Maintain a working assumption that the model is trying to be helpful and that bad output is fixable. That neutrality is what produces clean prompts.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.1. Rejecting performatively in interviews?
ai-interview.back: Saying "I reject AI code all the time" without specifics. The interviewer asks for an example, you do not have one, and you sound rehearsed. Always have a real example.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.2. Rejecting reactively, not deliberately?
ai-interview.back: Rejecting because the diff "feels off" without articulating why. That feeling is data, but it has to translate to a sentence. Otherwise you cannot prompt better next time.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.3. Confusing rejection with failure?
ai-interview.back: A rejected diff is not a failure. It is the review step working as intended. If your tool produces a wrong diff and you catch it, the system worked. The failure case is the wrong diff that ships.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/03-rejecting-ai-output.md: 11.4. Rejecting because of taste alone?
ai-interview.back: Sometimes you reject because the diff is "ugly." That is allowed, but be honest about it. Style preferences are not architectural concerns. Distinguish them when explaining your rejection.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-03-prompt-mastery/README.md: 'The interviewer can tell within five lines of a prompt wheth...'
ai-interview.back: The interviewer can tell within five lines of a prompt whether you respect the craft of prompting or treat it as a magic spell. This chapter teaches you to demonstrate the craft.

ai-interview.back: Should this handle the empty input case as zero or as an error?" "Should I prioritize readability or performance for this scale?" "Are there existing patterns in the codebase I should follow, or is this a greenfield decision?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 'My plan is: define the type first, then write a pure functio...'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 'OK, my prompt: 'Define a TypeScript interface for [thing]. S...'
ai-interview.back: OK, my prompt: 'Define a TypeScript interface for [thing]. Strict — no any. Add a comment explaining the invariant about [whatever]. Single file. No tests yet.'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 'Now I want to add a render function. I'll prompt for it expl...'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 'OK, it added the function. The signature looks right. The bo...'
ai-interview.back: OK, it added the function. The signature looks right. The body... iterates the array, accumulates... yep, that handles the happy path. But it does not handle the empty input case I asked about. I will reject this and re-prompt.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 1.3. AI-Augmented Take-Home?
ai-interview.back: > "Build this small project. AI is fine. Submit a PR within 48 hours plus a 5-minute Loom walking through your decisions."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 1 (5–10 seconds): Re-state the problem?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, so you want a function that takes [input] and returns [output], and the constraints are [constraint 1] and [constraint 2]. Did I understand correctly?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 3 (20–40 seconds): State your approach in plain English?
ai-interview.back: > "My plan is: define the type first, then write a pure function that handles the happy path, then add the edge cases I just asked about, then write three unit tests covering those cases."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Step 4 (40–60 seconds): Open the AI tool, write the first prompt deliberately?
ai-interview.back: Do not paste. Type. Out loud, narrate the components from Lesson 3.1:

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 3.1. The pre-prompt narration?
ai-interview.back: Before each prompt, narrate what you are about to ask and why.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.1. After the prompt produces output?
ai-interview.back: Read it line by line. Out loud. Two of three candidates skim and accept; you read and verify. The interviewer's notes will reflect that.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.2. Before accepting a refactor?
ai-interview.back: If the AI is changing existing code, always slow down. "Let me read the diff before I accept." The interviewer hears: this person catches accidental changes. Senior signal.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.3. Right before you say "I think we are done"?
ai-interview.back: Take 30 seconds. Re-read the requirement. Walk through one input mentally. Catch one off-by-one or null-handling miss before the interviewer does. Often the difference between an offer and a "not quite this round."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 4.4. When the interviewer says "Hmm..."?
ai-interview.back: If the interviewer makes a hesitant noise, stop typing. Look up. Say: "You sound like you might be seeing something. Want to share?" This converts a potential pothole into a teaching moment for them and survives many close calls.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 5.2. The "skip the constraint" speed-prompt?
ai-interview.back: You skip the constraints because you want to move fast. The output sprawls. You spend 10 minutes cleaning up. Net negative.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 5.3. The fix?
ai-interview.back: Before each prompt under observation, count to three in your head. "Three. Two. One." Three seconds of breathing space between thinking and typing. It feels long. It is fast.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: What NOT to do?
ai-interview.back: - Apologize for the AI. "Sorry, the AI is being weird." Sounds like deflection. - Argue with the AI. "No, I told you not to..." — pointless on screen. - Panic-accept. The diff is wrong but you accept it because you do not know what to do. - Switch to typing it yourself silently. The narration died; the interviewer is now staring at your hands.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: What to do?
ai-interview.back: 1. Pause. "OK, this is not what I asked for." 2. Diagnose out loud. "It overshot — refactored the parent. I will reject this and re-prompt with explicit scope." 3. Re-prompt. Narrate the new prompt's added constraint. 4. Move on.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Minute 0:00–1:00 — Re-state and clarify?
ai-interview.back: > "So I need a function: input is a list of users, output is the top 3 by signup recency, filtering out unverified. Two questions: how is signup recency stored — timestamp, ISO string, Date? And if there are fewer than 3 verified users, do I return what I have or throw?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: Minute 17:00–25:00 — Debug?
ai-interview.back: The failing test: returns 3 users newest-first. Debug. The output is correct in count but the order is oldest-first. Look at the function. The sort is wrong direction.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.1. Over-narration?
ai-interview.back: Talking nonstop. The interviewer cannot think. Pause every 30–60 seconds. Silence is allowed.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.2. Under-narration?
ai-interview.back: Typing silently. The interviewer cannot read your process. Default to narrating each prompt and each diff review.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.3. Selling the AI instead of yourself?
ai-interview.back: Saying things like "watch how fast this is with AI". The interview is about you, not the tool. Talk about your decisions; the AI is in the background.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md: 12.4. Hiding the AI?
ai-interview.back: Some candidates open the AI panel offscreen and quietly use it. The interviewer notices. Be visible. The AI is part of your toolset; do not be secretive.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 'Walk me through what you do when you discover the AI's recen...'
ai-interview.back: Walk me through what you do when you discover the AI's recent change broke something.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 'Your last response had a bug. The bug was [specific behavior...'
ai-interview.back: Your last response had a bug. The bug was [specific behavior]. Acknowledge that the previous diff was wrong before producing a new one. Show me where you went wrong.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 'What logging would you add to make sure this does not regres...'
ai-interview.back: What logging would you add to make sure this does not regress silently?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.1. The plausible-but-fabricated API?
ai-interview.back: The code calls a method that does not exist. Or it calls a method with the wrong parameter order. Or it imports a function from a module that exports something almost-but-not-quite the same name.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.2. The off-by-one in array logic?
ai-interview.back: AI is uneven on array boundary conditions. It will write for (let i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) or arr.slice(0, n - 1) when you wanted slice(0, n). Often the test for the happy path passes, and the boundary fails.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.3. The "fixed" test?
ai-interview.back: The test was failing. The AI made it pass — by changing the assertion, not the implementation. Tests are now green; behavior is still wrong.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.4. The "almost-correct" regex / SQL / glob?
ai-interview.back: AI is excellent at producing regexes and SQL that look right and silently mismatch. Common: a regex that allows or rejects one extra character; an SQL JOIN that should be LEFT JOIN; a glob that matches *.tsx when the codebase has .ts and .tsx mixed.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.5. The deprecated pattern?
ai-interview.back: The code uses an API that was correct in 2022 and incorrect now. Common in fast-moving frameworks: Next.js pages/ patterns in an App Router project, React componentWillMount, deprecated Node APIs.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.6. The silently-swallowed error?
ai-interview.back: AI loves try { ... } catch (e) {} for any operation that could throw. The error gets swallowed. The bug surfaces three layers downstream as a confused null.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 3.7. The unintended state mutation?
ai-interview.back: AI sometimes mutates objects passed in as arguments instead of returning new ones. Real-world bug: a Redux reducer that mutates state, breaking React's diff detection.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 4.1. The first sentence — declare what you are doing?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, before I run anything, let me read what the code says it does."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 4.3. The confirmation — run a tiny experiment?
ai-interview.back: > "Quickest way to test that — call the function with [specific input], observe [specific output]."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 1 — Read?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, the function takes picks and winners arrays, iterates with i = 0; i <= picks.length, and adds 3 for matches, 1 otherwise. Returns total."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 2 — Hypothesis (plant the flag)?
ai-interview.back: > "My first hypothesis: the loop condition is <= instead of <. That's an off-by-one. It runs one extra iteration where both picks[i] and winners[i] are undefined, undefined === undefined is true, so it adds 3. Test expects 7, got 8. The 8 = 2 correct + 1 wrong + 1 phantom 'correct' from the off-by-one. Hypothesis matches."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 3 — Confirm?
ai-interview.back: > "Let me confirm — running the test mentally: i=0, A===A, +3. i=1, B===X, +1. i=2, C===C, +3. i=3, undefined===undefined, +3. Total: 10. But the test says 8. So my hypothesis is wrong about the magnitude."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 4 — Re-read more carefully?
ai-interview.back: > "OK — the loop condition runs i = 0, 1, 2, 3. At i=3, picks[3] and winners[3] are both undefined. The condition picks[i] === winners[i] is undefined === undefined === true, so we add 3. That should give us 10, not 8. So either I'm misreading or the test framework cuts something off..."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Step 5 — Fix and narrate?
ai-interview.back: > "Two options for the fix. [A] Change <= to <. [B] Add a length-equality precondition with a thrown error. A is the minimum fix. B is more defensive but assumes the caller might pass mismatched arrays. The function's contract isn't documented, so I'll do A and add a comment that the caller is responsible for matched lengths."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Re-prompt when?
ai-interview.back: - The bug requires a structural change you do not want to make manually. - The diff would be larger than what is reasonable to type by hand. - The original prompt was missing a constraint and adding it would fix the issue cleanly. - You are short on time and the AI's pattern-matching beats your typing.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: Fix yourself when?
ai-interview.back: - The bug is a single character / single line. - The bug is in code the AI keeps re-introducing despite your prompts. - You need to ensure no surrounding code changes (high blast radius). - You can explain the fix faster than you can write a precise prompt for it.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: The interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "My rule: re-prompt for structural changes I would otherwise have to think hard about. Fix manually for one-character bugs and for high-blast-radius code where I need precision. The rule is not 'always one or the other'; it is 'use the right tool for the change size and blast radius.'"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: When AI-debugging works?
ai-interview.back: - You give the AI new information it did not have. ("Here is the failing test message: ...") - You give it a tight scope. ("Patch only the loop condition in lib/scoring.ts.") - You explicitly tell it to revert its previous attempt and start over.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: When AI-debugging fails?
ai-interview.back: - You vaguely ask "fix it" — the AI guesses, often the same way as before. - You forgot to mention what's wrong. The AI sees green tests and thinks you are confused. - You let the AI keep iterating without verifying each iteration.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.1. The seven-signature drill?
ai-interview.back: Take a working file from your codebase. Have a friend introduce one of the seven failure modes from section 3 (without telling you which). Time yourself debugging. Repeat with each of the seven.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.2. The fake-AI-output drill?
ai-interview.back: Find a small open-source repo. Pretend an LLM wrote it. Read it as if you were debugging — annotate any place that smells like one of the seven signatures. Compare your annotations to the project's git log; were the warts there real, or did you imagine them?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 10.3. The recorded-debug drill?
ai-interview.back: Same setup as Lesson 4.1. Record yourself debugging. Watch back. Note every silent stretch over 15 seconds. Each silent stretch is a missed narration opportunity.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.1. Skipping the "read first"?
ai-interview.back: Under stress, candidates jump straight to running. You miss bugs that the code itself reveals. Read first, run second.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.2. Over-trusting your hypothesis?
ai-interview.back: Two minutes spent defending a wrong hypothesis is two minutes wasted. The 30-second confirmation rule prevents this — if you cannot confirm in 30 seconds, the hypothesis is probably wrong.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.3. Fixing without testing the fix?
ai-interview.back: You found a bug. You patched it. You celebrate. Always re-run. Sometimes the "fix" introduces a different bug. Until the test is green, you are not done.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/02-debugging-ai-bugs.md: 11.4. Ignoring the seven signatures?
ai-interview.back: If you spot a bug pattern in section 3, say its name. The interviewer hears: this person has seen this pattern before. Senior signal.

ai-interview.front: Can you list the eleven-item review checklist from memory?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you name the three categories of senior comment with an example each?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you name the three categories of non-senior comment to avoid?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you walk a panel through a small AI-generated PR with the structural-then-line-by-line-then-summary rhythm?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you handle the "AI-generated PRs from a teammate" cultural question without being dismissive?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 'The third live format: a panel opens a PR on the shared scre...'
ai-interview.back: The third live format: a panel opens a PR on the shared screen and says "review this." What they are testing is whether you have an AI-aware reviewing checklist that catches things their juniors miss.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 'Here is a PR one of our junior engineers submitted. They use...'
ai-interview.back: Here is a PR one of our junior engineers submitted. They used AI assistance. Review it as if you're the senior on the team. Walk us through your thinking.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 'Two files in the diff: the implementation and one test. Desc...'
ai-interview.back: Two files in the diff: the implementation and one test. Description matches scope. No new dependencies. Good baseline.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 'How do you talk to a teammate who consistently submits AI-ge...'
