ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 'What's your take on AI-powered code review tools? Greptile, ...'
ai-interview.back: What's your take on AI-powered code review tools? Greptile, CodeRabbit, GitHub's review suggestions, etc.?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.1. Does it do what was asked??
ai-interview.back: The most basic question. Read the PR description. Read the diff. Are they about the same thing? AI loves to do "more than asked"; juniors accept the bonus work.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.2. Does every API call point to a real, current API??
ai-interview.back: Hover over imports. Check that methods exist. AI fabricates plausibly-named methods. A 30-second sanity check on imports catches this most of the time.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.3. Does it respect the codebase's existing patterns??
ai-interview.back: Find a sibling file with similar responsibilities. Compare. AI imports its training data's style; your codebase has its own. Mismatch = future maintenance pain.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.4. Are there any one-character bugs hiding in plain sight??
ai-interview.back: Off-by-one, wrong comparison operator, missing await, accidentally-async function. AI produces these at a slightly higher rate than careful humans.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.5. Were tests added that test the new behavior, not just pass??
ai-interview.back: Open the test file. Read each test. Does it actually exercise the change? Common AI failure: tests that import the new code and assert on a trivial property, giving false confidence.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.6. Are there empty try/catch blocks??
ai-interview.back: AI loves swallowing errors. Search for catch in the diff. Every empty body is a flag.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.7. Are there any types??
ai-interview.back: In a TypeScript codebase, any is a smell. AI sometimes reaches for any instead of working out the correct type. Each one needs justification.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.8. Are there hardcoded values that should be parameters or constants??
ai-interview.back: Magic numbers, hardcoded strings, baked-in URLs. AI defaults to plausible values rather than asking. Each one is a refactor invitation.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.9. Was anything deleted that should not have been??
ai-interview.back: Read the diff with an eye on the minus signs. AI is capable of helpfully removing lines it deems unnecessary that turn out to be load-bearing.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.10. Does the code match the PR description??
ai-interview.back: If the description says "fix bug X" and the diff also adds an unrelated feature, that is two changes in one PR. Push back.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 2.11. Could you ship this if you owned it??
ai-interview.back: The final gut-check. Imagine the PR is yours. Imagine being on call when it breaks. Would you ship it as-is? If no, articulate why.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.1. The "this won't compose" comment?
ai-interview.back: > "This is fine in isolation, but useUserScores is going to be called inside <TeamRow>, which itself is rendered in a list of 50. That means 50 hook invocations per render. We should hoist this up."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.2. The "this hides a bug we'll see in production" comment?
ai-interview.back: > "The empty catch on line 47 will swallow the network error. We will not see the failure in our metrics. Either rethrow or log."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 3.3. The "future maintainer will struggle" comment?
ai-interview.back: > "This name useThing will not survive a year. In six months someone will grep for the actual feature and miss this entirely. Suggest useTeamScoreSubscription or similar."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 5.1. The opening?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, let me start with the high-level — read the PR description, then walk the diff. I'll call out things as I see them."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: Structural pass?
ai-interview.back: > "Two files in the diff: the implementation and one test. Description matches scope. No new dependencies. Good baseline."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: Summary?
ai-interview.back: > "To summarize: I'd block this PR on three things — the type, the comparator (which has two bugs), and the missing real tests. I'd note the empty catch and the hardcoded 3 as non-blocking but should-fix. Three blockers, two suggestions."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.1. Critique the code, not the coder?
ai-interview.back: The PR allegedly came from "a junior engineer." Do not say things like "this is sloppy" or "the AI clearly made a mess." Talk about the code in third-person, neutrally.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.2. Compliment what works?
ai-interview.back: When something is correct, say so. "The scope is appropriately small — that's good." It signals that you read for both wins and losses, not just losses.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 7.3. Pace your delivery?
ai-interview.back: A reviewer who barrels through eight comments in two minutes sounds harsh. A reviewer who takes a breath after each major comment, makes eye contact, and lets the panel acknowledge before moving on — that reviewer sounds senior.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.1. The salted PR drill?
ai-interview.back: Take a real, working PR from your repo (open-source or your own). Have a friend salt it with one of the AI failure-mode signatures from Lesson 4.2. Open it cold. Time yourself reviewing. Did you find the salt? How long did it take?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.2. The peer review?
ai-interview.back: Pair with another developer. Each of you submits a small AI-assisted PR to the other for review. Treat it as a real interview format — narrate aloud, written comments after. Switch roles. Watch how each of you reviews differently.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 11.3. The recorded review?
ai-interview.back: Open any open-source PR you do not have context for. Record yourself reviewing it for 10 minutes. Watch back. Note the gaps in your narration and the comments you made that lacked justification.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.1. Reading silently?
ai-interview.back: If the panel cannot hear you think, they will not credit you with thinking. Default to narration. Even "Reading the diff..." is enough to fill the air.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.2. Diving into the deepest detail first?
ai-interview.back: Some candidates immediately zoom into a regex or a comparator and start picking it apart. Resist. The structural pass first — "is the scope right? Are the right files touched?" — sets you up to be the reviewer the panel wants to hire.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.3. Performing skepticism?
ai-interview.back: Some candidates over-criticize because they think it makes them look senior. The opposite. The most senior reviewer says "this part is fine" the most often — they have calibrated the bar precisely.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/03-code-review-of-ai.md: 12.4. Forgetting to summarize?
ai-interview.back: You spent 10 minutes finding issues. The panel needs a 30-second wrap. Always summarize at the end. Count the blockers. Count the suggestions. Hand it back.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-04-live-scenarios/README.md: 'The interview formats are changing. "Solve this on a whitebo...'
ai-interview.back: The interview formats are changing. "Solve this on a whiteboard, no internet" is dying. "Build this with AI, share your screen" is the new normal. This chapter teaches you to dominate that format.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'You can use an LLM during this interview. When would you?...'

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 'If I asked GPT-5 to design Twitter for me, wouldn't I get a ...'
ai-interview.back: If I asked GPT-5 to design Twitter for me, wouldn't I get a reasonable answer?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.1. Domain modeling?
ai-interview.back: The shape of the data, the entities, the relationships, and the invariants. The AI can propose a schema. Choosing the schema — knowing what to denormalize, what to make immutable, what to version — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.2. Boundary placement?
ai-interview.back: Where do components, services, and modules end and begin? The AI can split a file at a sensible spot. Drawing the system's seams — knowing which transformation belongs at the API, which at the service, which at the DB — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.3. Failure-mode design?
ai-interview.back: What happens when this fails? The AI can add a try/catch. Designing for graceful degradation — knowing what should retry, what should fail closed, what should fail open, what should circuit-break — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.4. Trade-off ownership?
ai-interview.back: When two designs are equally valid, which ships? The AI can list trade-offs. Picking the trade-off — accepting that the choice will be questioned in 6 months when reality bites — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.5. Scale and cost?
ai-interview.back: Will this hold at 100x the current load? At 0.1x? Sizing the system, choosing the cost-vs-latency curve, knowing when premature optimization actually pays — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: 2.6. Migration and rollout?
ai-interview.back: How do you ship a change without breaking the world? The AI can produce a migration script. Designing the rollout — feature flags, dual-writes, dark launches, canary fleets — is yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What the AI does?
ai-interview.back: > "Define a Postgres schema for users, orders, and order line items."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "The AI gives me schemas that work for the demo. The schemas that work for five years require knowing which fields are point-in-time snapshots, which are current values, and which are derived. Those are domain decisions, not database decisions, and they have to come out of conversations with the people who own the business rules."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Generating the SQL once you've made the decisions. - Listing trade-offs of normalize-vs-denormalize. - Spotting missing indexes once you describe the access patterns.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Boundaries are the most expensive thing in any architecture — once a service boundary exists, it is almost impossible to undo. The AI moves files around for me. Choosing where the boundaries are, knowing which seams must never be crossed and which can flex — those decisions live with us for years and are probably 30 percent of what 'architecting' actually means."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Generating the boilerplate when a boundary is decided (interface, adapter, contract). - Spotting violations of an existing boundary in a PR. - Drafting a deprecation note when a boundary changes.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Most architectures' failure modes are designed by accident — whatever the original code happened to do. Designing failure modes deliberately means knowing which calls must succeed, which can drop, which can retry. The AI helps me write the retry loop once I've decided this should be a retry loop. The decision is upstream of the code."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Implementing retry logic once you've specified the retry policy. - Drafting alert rules from a runbook. - Listing the failure modes in a checklist that you then categorize.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
ai-interview.back: - Pick one. Accountably. - Document why you picked it, in a way that survives the next on-call rotation when someone wonders why we're not doing the obvious other thing. - Carry the explanation through stakeholder pushback ("can't we just...?") with confidence. - Revisit when conditions change. The AI does not know that the cost of option A doubled this quarter.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI is a fantastic trade-off-listing machine. It is, by definition, not a trade-off-making machine. The trade-off only becomes a trade-off when someone owns the consequences. The AI doesn't carry a pager. I do."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Surfacing trade-offs you might miss. - Estimating the cost / latency / complexity of each option. - Drafting the ADR (architecture decision record) once you've chosen.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
ai-interview.back: - Distinguish expected scale (1M users) from bursty scale (1M concurrent in 5 minutes during a marketing event) from degenerate scale (one bad actor). Each one demands a different design. - Decide which scaling strategies are worth it given your current size. Premature scale work is one of the most common forms of waste. - Trade off cost against latency at each layer. The AI doesn't know your CFO's tolerance. - Decide what to shed when overloaded. The AI does not own the customer's emotional response to a 503.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "The right scale design is rarely the maximum scale design. Most systems do not need horizontal sharding; most systems would benefit from a cache layer they don't have; most systems will fall over in ways the load test missed. Choosing the right level of scale investment for a given moment in a company's life — that's a judgment about people and budget, not just throughput. AI doesn't see those."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Sizing calculations once you've stated the load. - Comparing AWS vs GCP vs self-hosted costs at a stated load. - Sketching the failure modes of each scaling strategy.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: What only you do?
ai-interview.back: - Decide whether to dual-write to old and new for a period. - Decide whether to backfill the new column synchronously, asynchronously, or never. - Decide whether to feature-flag the new path. The AI doesn't know your flag system. - Decide whether to canary to 1%, 10%, 100%. The AI doesn't know your incident history. - Decide what rollback looks like. The AI's "if it fails just rollback" is naive — some changes are unrollable once any traffic has used them.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Senior soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "Migrations are where engineering meets reality. Every elegant migration on paper has a rollback story that gets told once and never written down. Designing the rollback before the rollout is the discipline. AI helps me with the script. The decision tree above the script is mine — and it's where most production incidents come from."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: - Generating the migration SQL or code. - Drafting the dual-write logic once you've chosen it. - Reviewing your runbook for missing steps.

And one more: is there real money on the platform, or is this a free social product?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: The interviewer's likely answer?
ai-interview.back: > "DAU 200k, peak 500k. Pick'em-style, like the snooker app you may have seen. No real money — it's a community product."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI does NOT help?
ai-interview.back: You don't ask the AI clarifying questions. You ask the interviewer. The clarification phase is human-to-human. Reaching for the AI here would signal you don't know who to talk to.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, given that scope, let me list the functional requirements. I'll bullet them and you can tell me if I miss anything."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: You do not ask the AI here either, but — if you are 50 minutes in and forgot something, you could quickly prompt:

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
ai-interview.back: > "Now the non-functional side. These shape the architecture more than the features do."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps?
ai-interview.back: You could sanity-check your latency assumptions with the LLM:

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, the boxes-and-arrows. I'll start with the data flow for the most performance-critical path — live scoring — and work outward."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What only humans do in this phase?
ai-interview.back: - Choose the stream technology. The AI can list Kafka vs SQS vs Redis Streams trade-offs. You pick — based on your team's familiarity, your AWS spend, your latency budget. - Choose the cache topology. Redis cluster? In-process? Edge cache? Each one has consequences for failure modes and dev experience. - Decide which writes go to the stream vs which are synchronous. Pick submission must be synchronous (user expects confirmation). Score updates can be async.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Why this is interview-grade?
ai-interview.back: - You named two strategies. - You picked one with a reason rooted in expected scale. - You stated the conditions under which you'd switch. - You noted that the migration is local — meaning your boundary placement was good.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Where the AI helps in deep dives?
ai-interview.back: This is the phase where you might genuinely reach for the LLM:

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
ai-interview.back: > "With time left, let me name the parts of this design I'd most worry about and how I'd mitigate each."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: Why this phase is the most senior phase?
ai-interview.back: Most candidates run out of steam after the deep dive and just answer follow-up questions. You volunteer the risks. That signals: I think about my own designs critically.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: What you say?
ai-interview.back: > "OK, summary in 30 seconds: the architecture is event-driven scoring, Redis-cached read paths, two boundaries — match service and scoring worker. The known weak points are stream backlog under burst, cold cache on Redis restart, and GDPR deletion of historic-leaderboard data. Things I haven't covered that I'd want to: notifications fan-out, social features, and the admin tooling itself. Want to dig into any of those, or any part of what I sketched?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.1. Over-reliance on the AI?
ai-interview.back: A candidate I watched once asked the AI eleven times in a 50-minute interview. Each ask was a small question. Cumulatively, the panel concluded the candidate couldn't make decisions without the AI. Limit yourself to two or three deliberate uses.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.2. Drawing without narrating?
ai-interview.back: Silent drawing is the worst possible mode. Even if your design is perfect, the panel can't see your reasoning. Talk.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.3. Skipping the clarification?
ai-interview.back: Jumping into design before clarification produces a design for the wrong problem. Five minutes of clarification saves twenty minutes of redesign.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md: 12.4. Forgetting to name the pillars?
ai-interview.back: Every system-design hour is a referendum on Lesson 5.1's six categories. If you finish without having explicitly named at least three of them, the panel may not have heard you cover them. Name them as you cover them. "This is a boundary-placement decision..." makes it visible.

ai-interview.back: Every senior interview eventually becomes a trade-off conversation. Not "what would you build?" — but "why this and not that?" This lesson teaches you the language, structure, and pitfalls of those conversations.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 1 — Restate the choice?
ai-interview.back: > "OK — the question is whether to use A or B for [purpose]. Let me make sure I understand both."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 2 — Name the axes that matter?
ai-interview.back: > "There are roughly four axes I'd evaluate this on: [axis 1], [axis 2], [axis 3], [axis 4]."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 3 — Score each option on each axis?
ai-interview.back: > "On [axis 1], A wins because [reason]. On [axis 2], B wins because [reason]. On [axis 3], roughly equal. On [axis 4], it depends on [unknown]."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 4 — Identify the deciding axis for this situation?
ai-interview.back: > "Given the scenario you described — [specific constraint] — I'd weight [axis] highest, because [reason]. That tips the decision toward A."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: Step 5 — State the decision and the reversibility?
ai-interview.back: > "So I'd start with A. If [observable signal], I'd revisit and consider migrating to B. The migration would cost roughly [X], so the cost of being wrong is bounded."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.1. Latency?
ai-interview.back: How fast does the user-facing response come back? p50, p95, p99.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.4. Complexity?
ai-interview.back: How hard is this for the team to build, debug, and operate? Junior-friendly vs senior-only.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.5. Reliability?
ai-interview.back: What's the failure surface? How often does it fall over? How fast does it recover?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 3.6. Consistency?
ai-interview.back: Strong, eventual, causal, monotonic. What's the user-visible behavior under concurrent updates?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.2. Microservices vs monolith?
ai-interview.back: > "The honest answer is that almost everyone over-builds microservices. Monolith wins on time-to-ship, debugging, and team-cohesion-cost. Microservices win on independent deployment, language flexibility, and bounded blast radius — but only if you have the operational chops for them. > > I'd default to monolith for a team under 30 engineers. Above that, I'd start carving out the parts where independent deploy or different runtime make a real difference — usually the parts at the edge of the system, like ingress or fan-out workers. That's pragmatic microservices, not the full lift."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.3. Server-side rendering vs client-side rendering?
ai-interview.back: > "Three considerations: time-to-first-content, SEO, and runtime cost. SSR wins on TTFC and SEO. CSR wins on per-request server cost — once the JS is loaded, the client does the work. > > For a fantasy app, the leaderboard pages benefit from SSR — fast first paint, SEO for shareable URLs. The interactive parts — pick submission, real-time scoring — are CSR-driven. Modern frameworks (Next, Remix, SvelteKit) let me mix the two per route. I'd lean SSR-by-default, hydrate-when-needed."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 4.4. Build vs buy?
ai-interview.back: > "The trap with 'build vs buy' is that engineers default to 'build because we'll customize it perfectly,' and the customization never ends up mattering. The honest axis: how core is this to our differentiation? > > Auth: buy. Stripe / Clerk / Auth0 — never going to be our differentiator, the threat model is hard, every team that's tried has regretted it. > > Scoring engine: build. It's the heart of the product. We'll iterate on it for years. > > Email delivery: buy. SendGrid / Postmark. > > General rule: buy commodity infrastructure, build domain logic."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.2. "Premature [X] is the root of all evil."?
ai-interview.back: Use when: someone proposes a complexity that won't pay off for a while. Substitute optimization, abstraction, microservicing, generalization.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.3. "Engineering is choosing what to be wrong about."?
ai-interview.back: Use when: a perfectionist on the panel keeps pushing for the most-correct option without weighing costs.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.4. "Every architecture has a customer."?
ai-interview.back: Use when: discussing trade-offs that pit developer convenience against user experience.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.5. "I'd write the ADR before I write the code."?
ai-interview.back: Use when: discussing how to document a decision. ADR = architecture decision record.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.6. "What's the cost of being wrong here?"?
ai-interview.back: Use when: someone is over-thinking a low-stakes decision. Forces them to articulate why this one matters.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.7. "If we had to deprecate this in two years, what would that look like?"?
ai-interview.back: Use when: discussing extensibility / boundary placement. The answer reveals coupling.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 5.8. "The hard part isn't choosing — it's communicating the choice to the team."?
ai-interview.back: Use when: the panel asks how you'd roll out a controversial decision. A favorite of staff interviewers.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 6.3. The "AI told me" trap?
ai-interview.back: > "Well, when I asked the LLM, it said B is preferred for this kind of thing."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 6.4. The buzzword trap?
ai-interview.back: > "We'd use a CQRS pattern with event sourcing and saga orchestration."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 7.2. The right response — engaging?
ai-interview.back: > "Interesting — I'd want to understand the case for the other option. What weight are you putting on which axis? My reasoning was [restate], but I might be missing something."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.1. Naming axes you can't actually score on?
ai-interview.back: If you say "latency is one axis" and then can't tell the interviewer what the latencies would be, you've over-reached. Only name axes you can score.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.2. Forgetting reversibility?
ai-interview.back: Every architectural decision has a reversibility cost. Name it. "If we're wrong, here's what migration looks like." Senior signal.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.3. Treating the trade-off as binary?
ai-interview.back: Often the answer is neither, or both with a clear seam. "We use SQL for the transactional core and Redis for the hot read paths" is a more sophisticated answer than "SQL" alone.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/03-trade-off-conversations.md: 12.4. Not bringing the AI into the conversation?
ai-interview.back: If you're in an AI-permitted interview and you never mentioned AI's role in trade-offs, the panel may wonder if you actually use it. One mention is enough. "In a real working context I'd ask the LLM to surface trade-offs I might have missed — but the decision still has to be mine."

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-05-system-design/README.md: 'System design interviews are the most important hour of the ...'
ai-interview.back: System design interviews are the most important hour of the loop, and AI changes them less than you think. This chapter teaches you to argue with the AI in the room, not for or against it.

ai-interview.back: The interviewer who asks "what risks do you take seriously?" is testing whether you have skin in the game. This lesson gives you the answer.

ai-interview.back: What do you do personally to avoid skill atrophy?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 'AI generated a function. You reviewed the diff. You committe...'
ai-interview.back: AI generated a function. You reviewed the diff. You committed it. It shipped a bug. Whose bug is it?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 'My personal commitment is: [specific behavior I do every tim...'
ai-interview.back: My personal commitment is: [specific behavior I do every time, no exceptions].

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 2.1. Hallucinations / fabrication?
ai-interview.back: The model produces confident output that is wrong. Invented APIs, made-up parameters, half-correct algorithms.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 2.2. Security risks in generated code?
ai-interview.back: Generated code that introduces vulnerabilities — SQL injection, XSS, credential leaks, insecure defaults.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 2.3. Intellectual property and licensing?
ai-interview.back: Generated code that resembles training-data copyright; license-incompatible snippets; sensitive data leaving your environment in prompts.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 2.4. Prompt injection and tool-use abuse?
ai-interview.back: Adversarial input that hijacks an AI agent into doing something it should not.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 2.5. Over-reliance and skill atrophy?
ai-interview.back: The team becomes worse at doing things the AI does for them; the long-term cost is a team that cannot reason about its own code.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: The 30-second answer?
ai-interview.back: > "Two related concerns. One: the model may regurgitate code that resembles training-data with a restrictive license. The mitigation is to be thoughtful with longer chunks the model produces verbatim — short snippets are usually fine, multi-hundred-line generations get extra scrutiny. Two: my prompts may leak my employer's IP into a third-party model's logs. The mitigation is to use enterprise-tier tools with no-train guarantees, or to redact before prompting. I treat prompts the way I treat Slack messages — written assuming someone could read them later."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: The interview setup?
ai-interview.back: > "AI generated a function. You reviewed the diff. You committed it. It shipped a bug. Whose bug is it?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: The right answer?
ai-interview.back: > "Mine. The AI is a tool. Tools have failure modes. Choosing to use a tool, knowing its failure modes, and deciding the output is good enough to ship — that's the engineer's job. There's no version of an incident response where 'the AI did it' is acceptable. If the bug is severe, my name goes on the postmortem, my action items are the followups, my conduct is reviewed. That's the deal. The accountability is not negotiable, and it's also the reason I'm being paid for this work in the first place."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: The wrong answers (avoid)?
ai-interview.back: - "Well, it depends — if the AI was..." No. Yours. - "It's a shared accountability between the model and the engineer..." No. The model can't sign your name on a postmortem. - "The team's, ultimately." True at one level, but at the PR level — yours.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: Why interviewers love this question?
ai-interview.back: Because the right answer is short, blunt, and reveals everything about a candidate's judgment. Anyone who tries to spread the blame fails the question. Anyone who owns it cleanly passes.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 12.1. Dismissive answers?
ai-interview.back: "Hallucinations are pretty rare these days." You sound naive. The interviewer gets a follow-up that exposes you. Treat each risk with respect, even the ones you rarely hit.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 12.2. Catastrophic answers?
ai-interview.back: "AI is a security nightmare; I barely use it." You sound hostile to the modern stack. The interviewer assumes you'll slow the team. Acknowledge risks; don't be governed by them.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 12.3. Missing the personal commitment?
ai-interview.back: If your answer to every risk is generic, you sound rehearsed. At least one risk should have a concrete personal commitment. "My rule is..." That makes you memorable.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md: 12.4. Trying to absolve yourself?
ai-interview.back: Anything that hints at "the AI did it, not me" is a lethal answer. Always own the diff. Always.

ai-interview.front: Can you list the seven cultural questions from memory?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you describe a healthy AI-using team and a sick one in concrete terms?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you handle the "your teammate's PRs are causing tech debt" question with peer-first instincts?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you deliver the "AI raises the floor for juniors; our job is to ensure it doesn't lower their ceiling" line naturally?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Can you reframe a challenging cultural question without taking the bait?
ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 'AI did not just change how individuals code. It changed how ...'
ai-interview.back: AI did not just change how individuals code. It changed how teams collaborate. This lesson is the cultural answer set — the questions interviewers ask increasingly often and few candidates rehearse for.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 'AI raises the floor for juniors. Our job is to ensure it doe...'
ai-interview.back: AI raises the floor for juniors. Our job is to ensure it doesn't lower their ceiling.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 'You have a teammate who consistently submits AI-generated PR...'
ai-interview.back: You have a teammate who consistently submits AI-generated PRs that you have to clean up. How do you handle it?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 'AI writes a fine first draft. The author has to make sure th...'
ai-interview.back: AI writes a fine first draft. The author has to make sure the message reflects intent — not just what changed, but why. Why is the part the AI doesn't know. So: AI assists, author owns. Same rule as the code itself.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: What changed?
ai-interview.back: Old pair programming: two engineers, one keyboard, taking turns driving and navigating. Both think; one types.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer?
ai-interview.back: > "Pair programming with AI in the room takes deliberate discipline. The temptation is to outsource the friction — the moments where two engineers would normally argue about an approach get short-circuited by 'let's just ask Claude.' That's where the value of pairing dies. The way I run pair sessions: we form a hypothesis ourselves before any prompt, we use the AI as a shared sounding board not as the decider, and we narrate our disagreements out loud. The AI is the third pair member, not the senior architect. Pairing well with AI is harder than pairing without, not easier."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: Why this answer lands?
ai-interview.back: It identifies the failure mode (outsourcing friction), names a discipline (form hypothesis first), and ends with a memorable line ("harder, not easier"). Drill it.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: What changed?
ai-interview.back: A reviewer can no longer assume the diff was hand-typed. Every PR could be AI-generated, AI-iterated, AI-reviewed before submission. The review needs to handle both cases.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer?
ai-interview.back: > "My review starts with: assume good intent, but read more carefully than I would have five years ago. The failure modes of AI-assisted PRs are different from purely-human ones — fabricated APIs, plausible patterns that don't match our codebase, helpful refactors of code the author didn't ask about. So my review checklist has AI-specific items now. I also try to leave the author room to explain their process. Sometimes the diff that looks suspicious is actually well-considered, and the author had to override the AI to land it. I want to hear that story before I assume the worst."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The senior follow-up move?
ai-interview.back: > "I never write 'is this AI-generated?' in a review comment. It puts the author on the defensive. If something looks off, I ask 'can you walk me through the reasoning here?' The answer tells me everything I need to know — about the AI's involvement and about the author's understanding. The conversation is the review."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The hard truth?
ai-interview.back: A junior on a modern team has a hard tradeoff: AI accelerates their feature output but slows the development of their intuitions. They ship faster than they can reason. The risk is a class of engineer who looks productive on metrics but cannot debug, cannot design, cannot reason about systems.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer?
ai-interview.back: > "My approach to junior onboarding has shifted. The first month, I have them do at least one task per week without AI. Not as a punishment — as a deliberate exercise in building reasoning muscles that AI can't build for them. We pair on those tasks. By month three I want them comfortable with AI tooling, but only after they have hand-typed enough code to feel the rhythm of the language. The goal is the same as it always was — produce engineers who can think — but the curriculum is different now because the temptation to bypass thinking is greater."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The interview soundbite?
ai-interview.back: > "AI raises the floor for juniors. Our job is to ensure it doesn't lower their ceiling."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The setup question?
ai-interview.back: > "You have a teammate who consistently submits AI-generated PRs that you have to clean up. How do you handle it?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer?
ai-interview.back: > "My take on policy is: be specific about what counts as AI-generated. We don't audit everyone's editor. What we do require: any AI-assisted PR is marked in the description, every line is the author's responsibility regardless of source, AI-generated tests must be reviewed for whether they actually test the new behavior, and AI-generated documentation must be edited for accuracy because the model will invent features that don't exist. The policy is short, but it pushes accountability back to the human and creates a record of when AI was used heavily."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: Why this lands with hiring managers?
ai-interview.back: Because it's the conversation they're having internally. They're worried about the same thing you just articulated. You sound like the person who solves their problem.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: A healthy team?
ai-interview.back: - Authors mark when a PR is heavily AI-assisted, without shame. - Reviewers ask "walk me through the reasoning" rather than "did the AI write this?" - Designs are still discussed in design docs by humans before code is written. - One pair-programming session per sprint runs without AI, by choice. - Postmortems include "what was the AI's role and what was ours?" without blame. - Juniors are paired with seniors on real work, not isolated with AI as their only collaborator. - The team has a shared, written prompt library and treats it as code.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: A sick team?
ai-interview.back: - AI use is hidden because the team treats it as cheating. - PRs ship that no human can fully explain. - Tests pass mysteriously after AI involvement and nobody asks why. - Postmortems blame the model. - Juniors do not get mentorship because seniors think AI provides it. - Design discussions happen inside individual prompts, not in shared docs. - There is no policy, and the absence of policy means inconsistent practice.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer (greenfield team)?
ai-interview.back: > "In a team that's not using AI yet, I would not lead with adoption. I would lead with a use-case audit — what tasks are eating the team's time that the right tooling could help with? Then propose a small, time-boxed pilot for one task category, with explicit success criteria. If it works, expand. If not, we learn. Adoption is a series of validated bets, not a top-down mandate."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The 30-second answer (messy team)?
ai-interview.back: > "In a team where AI use has gotten messy, I'd start by listening. What are people doing? What feels uncomfortable? Where are PRs landing that nobody fully owns? From the listening, write a one-page team policy — short, focused on accountability and review, not tool choice. Then pair the policy with one new ritual: a weekly 30-minute review club where we read recent PRs together, AI-touched and not, and discuss. Most cultural fixes are observation rituals more than rules."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The right framing?
ai-interview.back: - Be honest but generous. - Don't trash your current team. - Highlight the things you'd want to bring to the new team. - Highlight the things you want the new team to teach you.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: A template answer?
ai-interview.back: > "My current team is in the middle of adoption — some folks are heavy users, some are skeptical, no formal policy yet. I've been the unofficial advocate for one small thing: every AI-assisted PR gets a sentence in the description about what the prompt was. It's the smallest possible policy and it's already changed our review conversations. I'd want to bring that habit anywhere I went. What I'd want to learn from a more mature team is the tooling-investment side — system prompts, eval suites, prompt versioning. We don't do any of that yet."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: The right answer?
ai-interview.back: > "I have not. I have had hard performance conversations rooted in patterns of low-quality AI-generated code — the same conversation you'd have about any low-quality code, with the AI as part of the diagnosis. The fix in every case has been mentorship and review discipline, not termination. I think 'fired for AI misuse' is the wrong frame; the underlying issue is always quality, accountability, or trust. The tool is incidental."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 13.3. Skill stewardship?
ai-interview.back: The team protects the long-term capabilities of its engineers, especially juniors.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 14.1. Sounding like a policy memo?
ai-interview.back: If your answers are full of "the team must..." and "developers should...", you sound like HR. Use first-person and concrete examples. "On my last team..." is more vivid than "developers should..."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 14.2. Demonizing AI?
ai-interview.back: If every answer is about risks and downsides, you sound like a contrarian who'll slow the team. Balance with the upside. AI is a real productivity gain; you respect that.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 14.3. Missing the people angle?
ai-interview.back: These are culture questions. If your answer is mostly about tooling, you missed the question. Bring people into every answer — juniors, seniors, reviewers, managers.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/02-team-dynamics.md: 14.4. Pretending you've solved it?
ai-interview.back: If you claim your team has perfect AI culture, the interviewer doesn't believe you. Acknowledge ongoing work. "We're still figuring out X..." sounds far more credible than "we have all of this nailed."

ai-interview.back: Self-rate 1-5. If <4, re-read the lesson.

ai-interview.back: The interview is a narrative. You are the protagonist. AI is a supporting character. This lesson teaches you how to tell the story.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 'What's the one part of your codebase that everyone agrees ne...'
ai-interview.back: What's the one part of your codebase that everyone agrees needs a rewrite, but nobody has time for?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 'What's your team's relationship with AI tools today? What's ...'
ai-interview.back: What's your team's relationship with AI tools today? What's working, what isn't?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 'What does success in this role look like at the 90-day mark?...'
ai-interview.back: What does success in this role look like at the 90-day mark? What's the failure mode that worries you most?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 'AI raised the floor of what's possible. It has not lowered t...'
ai-interview.back: AI raised the floor of what's possible. It has not lowered the ceiling of what's required.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 'Re-open README.md any time you want a refresher on the chapt...'
ai-interview.back: Re-open README.md any time you want a refresher on the chapter map.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: The shape?
ai-interview.back: 1. Identity (10 sec): one sentence about who you are professionally. 2. Recent project (30 sec): the most relevant project, what you built, what was hard. 3. AI integration (20 sec): how AI fit in, what you owned, what it owned. 4. Pivot (15 sec): one trade-off you'd revisit. 5. The hook (15 sec): the one detail that ties it all together and asks for engagement.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Why the closing question matters?
ai-interview.back: The closing — "Would you like me to walk through..." — gives the interviewer a choice. They feel like a participant, not an audience. Always end an opening monologue with a forked invitation.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Foundation questions (Chapter 1)?
ai-interview.back: 1. "Tell me how you use AI in your day-to-day work." → 1.1 (30-second self-intro line) 2. "Are you a vibe coder?" → 1.1 (good vs bad version) 3. "What's the difference between AI generations of tools?" → 1.1 (the five generations)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: The big question (Chapter 2)?
ai-interview.back: 4. "Why do we still need human developers?" → 2.2 (seven pillars, 90-second master answer) 5. "AI is getting better fast — what about in 5 years?" → 2.2 (the bar for senior went up too) 6. "Tell me a time you didn't trust the AI." → 2.3 (case 1 / case 5 STAR story)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Prompt mastery (Chapter 3)?
ai-interview.back: 7. "Show me a recent prompt you wrote." → 3.1 (six-component analysis) 8. "Show me a recent prompt that produced bad output." → 3.3 (rejection story) 9. "Do you use system prompts / custom instructions?" → 3.1 (project-level system prompt answer)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Live skill (Chapter 4)?
ai-interview.back: 10. "Build a small feature with AI in front of me." → 4.1 (live pair-programming playbook) 11. "Debug this AI-generated bug." → 4.2 (four-step diagnosis flowchart) 12. "Review this AI-generated PR." → 4.3 (eleven-item checklist)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Architecture (Chapter 5)?
ai-interview.back: 13. "How would you design X — and where does AI fit?" → 5.2 (60-min walkthrough) 14. "What architectural decisions can the AI not make for you?" → 5.1 (six categories) 15. "How would you choose between A and B?" → 5.3 (five-step trade-off structure)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Risks and culture (Chapter 6)?
ai-interview.back: 16. "What's a risk you take seriously?" → 6.1 (one of five risks, 30-second answer) 17. "AI ships a bug — whose fault?" → 6.1 (Mine. The accountability answer.) 18. "How do you mentor a junior on an AI-using team?" → 6.2 (the floor/ceiling line)

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "Three things. First, [a type / boundary / abstraction] I'd revisit — [specific reason rooted in the project]. Second, [a tooling / testing / observability gap] I'd close — [specific reason]. Third, [a documentation / mentorship / cultural artifact] I'd add — [specific reason]."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 5.1. The codebase question?
ai-interview.back: > "What's the one part of your codebase that everyone agrees needs a rewrite, but nobody has time for?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 5.2. The AI-culture question?
ai-interview.back: > "What's your team's relationship with AI tools today? What's working, what isn't?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 5.3. The role-success question?
ai-interview.back: > "What does success in this role look like at the 90-day mark? What's the failure mode that worries you most?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Why these three work together?
ai-interview.back: - The first reveals the codebase. - The second reveals the culture. - The third reveals the role.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: Template?
ai-interview.back: > "One thing. I want to be transparent that I spent significant time preparing for this interview, including for the AI-related conversation, because I know it's where most candidates either oversell or undersell themselves. My honest pitch is: I'm a working engineer who treats AI as a real tool with real failure modes, and I've thought hard about the ones that matter. I'd be excited to bring that mindset to your team. Whatever you decide, thanks for the careful questions — they were good ones."

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 8.1. Sit forward when delivering the narrative?
ai-interview.back: Your body says: I am invested in this conversation. The interviewer mirrors and leans in. The temperature in the room rises slightly. Free signal.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 8.2. Hold a 2-second pause after major answers?
ai-interview.back: After the 90-second narrative, the seven-pillar answer, or the 30/60/90 promise — stop talking. Let the words land. Most candidates fill the silence with hedges ("...so yeah, that's roughly it..."). Don't. Silence is confidence.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 8.3. Make eye contact when delivering the punchline?
ai-interview.back: The most memorable lines — "My personal commitment is...", "AI raises the floor; it doesn't lower the ceiling...", "Mine." — should be delivered with eye contact, not while looking at your notes or your screen. The eye contact is the highlight reel.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 9.1. Camera position?
ai-interview.back: Camera at eye level. Not below. Below makes you look up the interviewer's nose; above is fine. Stack books.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 9.2. The "I lost connection" recovery?
ai-interview.back: If the connection drops, never panic. Reconnect. Open with: "Sorry — connection. Where were we — I had just finished talking about the trade-off in the leaderboard cache." That sentence demonstrates: you held context across the disruption. Senior signal even from a network hiccup.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 9.3. The screen-share discipline?
ai-interview.back: Before any technical exercise: close every tab and notification. Quit Slack. Close email. The interviewer sees what you screen-share — and what's behind it. One personal reminder peeking out the corner of the screen is enough to derail a candidate.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 11.1. Apologizing for AI use?
ai-interview.back: Anywhere in the closing — "I might have over-relied on AI..." — torches the offer. Frame AI use as professional craft, always.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 11.2. Making the 30/60/90 too aggressive?
ai-interview.back: "In 30 days I'd want to lead my own team..." sounds out of touch. The right pace is observation → contribution → ownership → leadership, with each phase taking a clear chunk of time.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 11.3. Missing the closing questions?
ai-interview.back: Saying "no, I think you covered everything" when asked if you have questions tells the panel: this person doesn't evaluate jobs. Always have three questions.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md: 11.4. Over-rehearsing the narrative?
ai-interview.back: If your 90-second pitch sounds memorized, it loses warmth. Rehearse for structure, not for words. Vary the wording on each delivery.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/README.md: 'The last chapter of the course is the most personal. Risks, ...'
ai-interview.back: The last chapter of the course is the most personal. Risks, ethics, team dynamics, and the closing narrative that ties your candidacy together. Done well, this is the chapter the interviewer remembers most.

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/README.md: 'A 6-chapter course for engineers who use AI tools daily and ...'
ai-interview.back: A 6-chapter course for engineers who use AI tools daily and need to defend their craft in interviews.* This is the answer to the question every hiring manager is asking in 2026: "Why should I still hire human developers?

ai-interview.front: Complete / recall the soundbite from ai-interview/README.md: 'Candidates who use AI well are easy to spot. So are candidat...'
ai-interview.back: Candidates who use AI well are easy to spot. So are candidates who use it badly. The hard ones are candidates who use it badly but think they use it well. Those are the hires we regret.

ai-interview.back: Goal by Sunday: prompt mastery solid, can walk into a live pair-programming round and run the 60-second playbook.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 1 (60 min) — Orientation?
ai-interview.back: - Read ai-interview-course/README.md end to end. - Skim every chapter README to see the arc. - Open the live deployed React Snooker app — that's your portfolio example throughout this course. - Output: list the 3 questions you most want to answer well by Day 21.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 2 (60 min) — Chapter 1.1?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-01-foundations/01-what-changed.md. - Memorize the 5 generations of AI tooling and the 3 workflows. - Practice the 30-second self-intro out loud, twice. - Output: write down your real-life "AI generation" — which one are you actually on?

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 3 (60 min) — Chapter 1.2 + 1.3?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-the-skill-stack.md and 03-the-mindset-shift.md. - Identify the 5 layers of engineering work and which you're strongest / weakest at. - Output: 1 example from your own work for each of the 5 layers.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 4 (75 min) — Chapter 2.1 (The Big Question, part 1)?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-02-the-big-question/01-the-five-framings.md. - For each framing, practice the opening 15 seconds of your response. - Output: pick the framing you find hardest and write a full answer to it.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 5 (90 min) — Chapter 2.2 (THE centerpiece)?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-the-seven-pillars.md. - Memorize all 7 pillars by name. - Practice the 90-second master answer 3 times, looking up only as needed. - Output: deliver the master answer end-to-end without notes; record it.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 6 (75 min) — Chapter 2.3?
ai-interview.back: - Read 03-real-world-cases.md. - Fill in the STAR worksheet at the bottom with 2-3 of your own real cases. - Practice telling one case story in 60 seconds. - Output: 3 STAR stories in your notes file.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 7 (60 min) — Week 1 review?
ai-interview.back: - Re-read cheat-sheets/ai-ch01.md and cheat-sheets/ai-ch02.md. - 30-minute flashcard session: tags self-quiz and concept, source chapter 1-2. - Practice 5 random questions in /interview-drill. - Output: weekly retro paragraph.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 8 (60 min) — Chapter 3.1?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-03-prompt-mastery/01-anatomy-of-a-prompt.md. - Pull out 3 real prompts from your last week of work; analyze each on the 6 components. - Output: rewrite one of them using all 6 components.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 9 (60 min) — Chapter 3.2?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-the-twelve-patterns.md. - Pick the 4 patterns most relevant to your daily work; memorize their names. - Output: deploy each of the 4 patterns once today in your actual editor.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 10 (60 min) — Chapter 3.3?
ai-interview.back: - Read 03-rejecting-ai-output.md. - Start a REJECTION_JOURNAL.md in your private notes; commit to logging 1 rejection/day for the next week. - Output: today's first rejection journal entry.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 11 (75 min) — Chapter 4.1?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-04-live-scenarios/01-live-pair-programming.md. - Memorize the 60-second playbook (the 4 spoken opening lines). - Practice the playbook out loud on a fake prompt ("build a function that..."). - Output: deliver the playbook from memory.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 12 (75 min) — Chapter 4.2?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-debugging-ai-bugs.md. - Memorize the 4-step diagnosis flowchart and the 7 failure signatures. - Output: write down 1 real bug you've debugged that matches one of the signatures.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 13 (60 min) — Chapter 4.3?
ai-interview.back: - Read 03-code-review-of-ai.md. - Memorize the 11-item review checklist. - Apply it to a real PR or a recent diff from your work. - Output: list of 3 things the checklist would have caught.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 14 (60 min) — Week 2 review?
ai-interview.back: - Re-read cheat-sheets/ai-ch03.md and cheat-sheets/ai-ch04.md. - 30-minute flashcard session: chapters 3-4. - Practice 10 random questions in /interview-drill. - Output: weekly retro.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 15 (60 min) — Chapter 5.1?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-05-system-design/01-what-humans-still-own.md. - Memorize the 6 categories of architecture decision. - Output: 1 personal example for each category.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 16 (90 min) — Chapter 5.2?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-design-with-ai-walkthrough.md. - Walk through the 60-minute design hour out loud, on paper, on your own. - Output: your own version of the architecture sketch.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 17 (60 min) — Chapter 5.3?
ai-interview.back: - Read 03-trade-off-conversations.md. - Memorize the 5-step trade-off structure and the 8 axes. - Pick 3 of the 12 rapid-drill trade-offs; deliver each in 60 seconds. - Output: 3 recorded 60-second trade-off answers.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 18 (75 min) — Chapter 6.1?
ai-interview.back: - Read chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/01-risks-and-accountability.md. - Memorize the 5 risks and the "Mine." accountability answer. - Write your 1-sentence personal commitment statement. - Output: personal commitment statement on a sticky note.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 19 (60 min) — Chapter 6.2?
ai-interview.back: - Read 02-team-dynamics.md. - Memorize the 7 cultural questions. - Practice the "AI raises the floor; it doesn't lower the ceiling" line until it sounds natural. - Output: a 60-second answer to "how would you onboard a junior?"

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 20 (90 min) — Chapter 6.3 (the closing)?
ai-interview.back: - Read 03-portfolio-narrative.md. - Write your 90-second portfolio narrative based on the React Snooker project. - Write your 3 closing questions to ask the interviewer. - Write your 30/60/90-day promise. - Output: all three in your notes, fully drafted.

ai-interview.front: In ai-interview/STUDY_PLAN.md: Day 21 (90 min) — Capstone?
ai-interview.back: - Run through the 18-question catalog (chapter-06-behavioral-and-closing/03-portfolio-narrative.md). - For each, deliver a clean answer in 60-90 seconds. - Do a full mock-interview session in /interview-drill — 10 random questions, timed. - Output: a self-graded scorecard, plus a sticky note of the 3 questions you most need to keep drilling.
